# MuleSoft Vibes for Anypoint Code Builder

> For the full documentation index, see: https://docs.mulesoft.com/llms.txt

MuleSoft Vibes is the natural-language assistant in Anypoint Code Builder for API specifications,
integrations, and related tasks by using the built-in MuleSoft MCP Server and optional custom MCP
servers. The end-to-end journey for MuleSoft Vibes consists of various tasks, each with links to
relevant content to assist you in completing them.

<table><colgroup><col> <col> <col></colgroup><tbody><tr><td><div><div><div><img src="../reuse/_images/lm_start.png" alt="Learn about MuleSoft Vibes"></div></div><div><p><span>Learn About MuleSoft Vibes</span></p></div><div><p>MuleSoft Vibes is a purpose-built assistant for the development lifecycle, available directly in Anypoint Code Builder. Build, deploy, and manage your projects using natural language prompts.</p></div><div><ul><li><p><a href="../anypoint-code-builder/mulesoft-vibes#capabilities">Understand capabilities in your workspace</a></p></li><li><p><a href="../anypoint-code-builder/mulesoft-vibes#related-ai">Compare with broader MuleSoft AI in related resources</a></p></li><li><p><a href="../mulesoft-mcp-server/">Review MuleSoft MCP Server</a></p></li><li><p><a href="../anypoint-code-builder/vibes-checkpoints">Track workspace changes with checkpoints</a></p></li><li><p><a href="../anypoint-code-builder/vibes-conversation-history#browse-conversation-history">Browse conversation history</a></p></li></ul></div></div></td><td><div><div><div><img src="../reuse/_images/lm_analyze_1.png" alt="Get started with MuleSoft Vibes"></div></div><div><p><span>Get Started</span></p></div><div><p>Set up the IDE, confirm permissions, then open MuleSoft Vibes and start prompting with confidence.</p></div><div><ul><li><p><a href="../anypoint-code-builder/start-acb">Set up and access the web or desktop IDE</a></p></li><li><p><a href="../anypoint-code-builder/start-workspaces">Organize workspace structure and folder levels</a></p></li><li><p><a href="../anypoint-code-builder/start-configure-permissions#permissions">Configure Anypoint Code Builder permissions</a></p></li><li><p><a href="../anypoint-code-builder/vibes-get-started#use-mulesoft-vibes">Use MuleSoft Vibes</a></p></li></ul></div></div></td><td><div><div><div><img src="../reuse/_images/lm_develop_1.png" alt="Design APIs" width="rules" height="and automation"></div></div><div><p><span>Design APIs, Rules, and Automation</span></p></div><div><p>Shape API specs with AI, attach context, tune Vibes settings, and standardize output with rules, workflows, and commands.</p></div><div><ul><li><p><a href="../anypoint-code-builder/vibes-api-ai-create-spec#design-spec-ai">Generate and refine specs</a></p></li><li><p><a href="../anypoint-code-builder/vibes-api-ai-create-spec#rules-workflows">Define rules and workflows</a></p></li><li><p><a href="../anypoint-code-builder/vibes-api-ai-create-spec#context-files">Attach requirements and outputs</a></p></li><li><p><a href="../anypoint-code-builder/vibes-api-ai-create-spec#settings">Control confirmations and file access</a></p></li><li><p><a href="../anypoint-code-builder/vibes-workflows-commands#add-workflows-or-rules">Add workflows and rules</a></p></li><li><p><a href="../anypoint-code-builder/vibes-workflows-commands#add-commands">Invoke slash shortcuts</a></p></li></ul></div></div></td></tr><tr><td><div><div><div><img src="../reuse/_images/lm_build_1.png" alt="Build integration projects"></div></div><div><p><span>Build Integration Projects</span></p></div><div><p>Attach context to MuleSoft Vibes, open the assistant for integrations, and scaffold an integration project.</p></div><div><ul><li><p><a href="../anypoint-code-builder/vibes-get-started#provide-mulesoft-vibes-with-context">Attach folders and files</a></p></li><li><p><a href="../anypoint-code-builder/vibes-create-integrations#access-dev-agent">Open the assistant for creating integrations</a></p></li><li><p><a href="../anypoint-code-builder/vibes-create-integrations#create-integration-project">Scaffold a project</a></p></li></ul></div></div></td><td><div><div><div><img src="../reuse/_images/lm_code_1.png" alt="Resume work" width="prompt" height="and improve output"></div></div><div><p><span>Resume Work, Prompt, and Improve Output</span></p></div><div><p>Pick up tasks from conversation history, write and verify prompts, reuse example patterns, and improve output when results are inconsistent.</p></div><div><ul><li><p><a href="../anypoint-code-builder/vibes-conversation-history#browse-conversation-history">Reopen tasks from browse conversation history</a></p></li><li><p><a href="../anypoint-code-builder/troubleshoot-generative-ai#_unexpected_results_when_using_mulesoft_vibes">Improve inconsistent output</a></p></li><li><p><a href="../anypoint-code-builder/vibes-create-integrations#craft-ai-prompt">Write your prompt</a></p></li><li><p><a href="../anypoint-code-builder/vibes-create-integrations#complete-gen-flow">Apply and verify results</a></p></li><li><p><a href="../anypoint-code-builder/vibes-prompt-examples#_order_management">Use example prompts</a></p></li></ul></div></div></td><td><div><div><div><img src="../reuse/_images/lm_integrate_1.png" alt="Configure and manage MCP servers"></div></div><div><p><span>Configure and Manage MCP Servers</span></p></div><div><p>Install and tune MCP servers so MuleSoft Vibes can call the right tools with the right approvals.</p></div><div><ul><li><p><a href="../anypoint-code-builder/vibes-mcp-server">Configure MCP server</a></p></li><li><p><a href="../anypoint-code-builder/vibes-mcp-server#add-an-mcp-server">Add an MCP server</a></p></li><li><p><a href="../anypoint-code-builder/vibes-mcp-server#add-and-manage-custom-mcp-servers">Add and manage custom MCP servers</a></p></li></ul></div></div></td></tr></tbody></table>

## See Also

- [Anypoint Code Builder Overview](../anypoint-code-builder/)

# Understand Mule Credits Usage and Rates

> For the full documentation index, see: https://docs.mulesoft.com/llms.txt

A Mule Credit is a fungible currency that allows you to draw down from a single capacity pool as you
consume services across MuleSoft. Instead of managing different usage types for all products, you
purchase a package of credits to use flexibly across the entire MuleSoft platform. As you use these
capabilities, Anypoint Platform deducts the corresponding credit amounts from your available
balance.

Mule Credits apply to your entire Anypoint Platform organization and you canât manage them per
business group. Use Mule Credits before the `Order End Date` set in the **Usage Details** table on
your **Order Form** because rollovers are not permitted. To see which usage types your subscription
includes, refer to your **Order Form**, **Subscription Plan**, or **Features and Entitlement
Document**.

## Mule Credits by Subscription Plan

The usage types available to you depend on your subscription plan.

<table><colgroup><col> <col></colgroup><thead><tr><th>Subscription Plan</th><th>Available Usage Types</th></tr></thead><tbody><tr><td><p><strong>MuleSoft Integration</strong></p></td><td><p>MuleSoft Flows, MuleSoft Messages, Private Spaces, Network Connections, Load Balancers</p></td></tr><tr><td><p><strong>MuleSoft Automation</strong></p></td><td><p>IDP Pages, Flow Integration Tasks</p></td></tr><tr><td><p><strong>MuleSoft AI &amp; API Management</strong></p></td><td><p>Managed Assets, Gateway Requests, Private Spaces, Network Connections</p></td></tr><tr><td><p><strong>MuleSoft Agent Fabric</strong></p></td><td><p>MuleSoft Flows, MuleSoft Messages, Managed Assets, Gateway Requests, Private Spaces, Network Connections</p></td></tr><tr><td><p><strong>MuleSoft All-Access</strong></p></td><td><p>All usage types: MuleSoft Flows, MuleSoft Messages, Managed Assets, Gateway Requests, IDP Pages, Flow Integration Tasks, Private Spaces, Network Connections, Load Balancers</p></td></tr><tr><td><p><strong>MuleSoft All-Access License</strong></p></td><td><p>All usage types: MuleSoft Flows, MuleSoft Messages, Managed Assets, Gateway Requests, IDP Pages, Flow Integration Tasks, Private Spaces, Network Connections, Load Balancers</p></td></tr></tbody></table>

> [!NOTE] MuleSoft All-Access License supports pre-commit and unlimited usage models.

## Usage Types

Each usage type (also called a meter) tracks a specific dimension of platform consumption. Credit
rates vary by monthly consumption volume and reset on the first day of each calendar month. You
consume credits at the same rate across all environments, including production, pre-production,
sandbox, and design. For the full tiered rate table, see [Rate Cards](#rate-cards).

<table><colgroup><col> <col></colgroup><thead><tr><th>Usage Type</th><th>Description</th></tr></thead><tbody><tr><td><p><strong>MuleSoft Flows</strong></p></td><td><p>A flow is a sequence of logical operations configured within the XML <code>&lt;flow/&gt;</code> element of a Mule application. The runtime report tracks flows in deployed and running Mule applications that contain a Mule event source or APIkit route requests.</p><p>Mule apps in production environments typically use multiple flows and subflows to divide the app into functional modules or for error-handling purposes. For example, one flow can receive a record and transform data into a given format that another flow processes.</p><p>To calculate usage, MuleSoft tracks the number of flows across all business groups, environments, and applications on an hourly basis. For every 24-hour period starting at 00:00 UTC, MuleSoft identifies the hour with the highest flow count as the daily max flow. Based on your contract term, MuleSoft then aggregates these daily maximums over the month or year to determine final usage.</p></td></tr><tr><td><p><strong>MuleSoft Messages</strong></p></td><td><p>The total volume of data containers processed by the runtime. To track message usage, the runtime report counts the number of times a Mule event source triggers a message. To calculate usage, MuleSoft aggregates the number of messages daily (UTC time) and monthly. After a Mule event source triggers a message, the report doesnât track changes to the message because the applicationâs flows process it internally.</p></td></tr><tr><td><p><strong>Managed Assets</strong></p></td><td><p>An asset (API, Agent, MCP Server, or LLM) counts as managed if it has at least one active instance in <a href="https://docs.mulesoft.com/api-manager/latest/latest-overview-concept">API Manager</a> deployed to a <a href="https://docs.mulesoft.com/mule-gateway/mule-gateway-capabilities-mule4" target="_blank" rel="noopener">Mule Gateway</a> or <a href="https://docs.mulesoft.com/gateway/latest/" target="_blank" rel="noopener">Omni Gateway</a>. Anypoint Platform counts usage per business group per day; if an asset has active instances across multiple business groups, it counts as one asset per group.</p><p>Multiple instances within the same business group donât incur extra charges. Anypoint Platform deducts credits as long as the asset status is Active in API Manager, even if there are no calls to the asset.</p></td></tr><tr><td><p><strong>Gateway Requests</strong></p></td><td><p>Any request that <a href="https://docs.mulesoft.com/gateway/latest/" target="_blank" rel="noopener">Omni Gateway</a> receives. MuleSoft measures usage as the sum of all processed requests, regardless of response status.</p></td></tr><tr><td><p><strong>IDP Pages</strong></p></td><td><p>The number of individual document pages that Intelligent Document Processing (IDP) processes in <a href="https://docs.mulesoft.com/idp/" target="_blank" rel="noopener">MuleSoft IDP</a> or <a href="https://blogs.mulesoft.com/news/mulesoft-for-flow-intelligent-document-processing/" target="_blank" rel="noopener">MuleSoft for Flow: IDP</a>. MuleSoft counts each page within a document as a separate unit, and charges only for successful LLM responses.</p></td></tr><tr><td><p><strong>Flow Integration Tasks</strong></p></td><td><p>Individual units of work or actions (reading, creating, or updating a record) executed within <a href="https://help.salesforce.com/s/articleView?id=platform.automate_flow_get_started_mfi.htm&amp;type=5" target="_blank" rel="noopener">MuleSoft for Flow: Integration</a>.</p></td></tr><tr><td><p><strong>Private Spaces</strong></p></td><td><p>A <a href="https://docs.mulesoft.com/cloudhub-2/ch2-private-space-about" target="_blank" rel="noopener">Private Space</a> is a dedicated, isolated environment in CloudHub 2.0 for running your apps. You consume credits based on the total number of provisioned Private Spaces per day. After you deprovision a Private Space, it stops consuming credits from the next calendar date onwards.</p></td></tr><tr><td><p><strong>Network Connections</strong></p></td><td><p>Network connections allow you to securely connect corporate data centers and on-premises applications to CloudHub 1.0 and <a href="https://docs.mulesoft.com/cloudhub-2/ch2-private-space-about#private-network" target="_blank" rel="noopener">CloudHub 2.0</a> using a VPN or AWS Transit Gateway. You consume credits based on the total number of active network connections per day. After you deprovision a network connection, it stops consuming credits from the next calendar date onwards.</p></td></tr><tr><td><p><strong>Load Balancers</strong></p></td><td><p><a href="https://docs.mulesoft.com/cloudhub/cloudhub-dedicated-load-balancer" target="_blank" rel="noopener">Dedicated Load Balancers</a> used to distribute incoming traffic across multiple workers or instances. You consume credits based on the total number of provisioned load balancers per day. After you deprovision a load balancer, it stops consuming credits from the next calendar date onwards.</p></td></tr></tbody></table>

## Rate Cards

You consume Mule Credits at the applicable multiplier rate based on the usage type identified in the
[Mule Credit Rate Card](https://www.salesforce.com/en-us/wp-content/uploads/sites/4/documents/mulesoft/Mule_Credit_Rate_Card.pdf).

If your usage exceeds your contracted amount, MuleSoft sends a monthly overage invoice to your
primary billing contact at your standard contracted rate. Contact your account team with any
questions about your contract or billing.

## Viewing Usage and Billing

Mule Credits usage, monthly statements, and overage billing are centralized in Salesforce Digital
Wallet. If you have an Anypoint Platform organization, you can also view your usage and entitlement
details in Anypoint Usage Reports for cross-platform transparency.

To view your Mule Credits consumption:

1.  From the **App Launcher**, click **Digital Wallet**.
2.  Locate the **Mule Credits** consumption card in the **Consumption Cards** tab.

Digital Wallet is available in Lightning Experience in Enterprise and Unlimited editions, and
requires the View Consumption user permission.

### Digital Wallet Features at a Glance

<table><colgroup><col> <col></colgroup><thead><tr><th>Feature</th><th>For More Information</th></tr></thead><tbody><tr><td><p>Monitor and analyze near-real-time usage for your consumption-based products.</p></td><td><p><a href="https://help.salesforce.com/s/articleView?id=xcloud.wallet_monitor_usage.htm&amp;language=en_US&amp;type=5" target="_blank" rel="noopener">Monitor Usage for Consumption-Based Products in Digital Wallet</a></p></td></tr><tr><td><p>View the Consumption Analytics dashboard, which includes Tableau Next visualizations that leverage standard consumption tags. Visualizations are interactive, allowing you to instantly transform your view, drill down into details, and optimize business planning with consumption forecasting.</p></td><td><p><a href="https://help.salesforce.com/s/articleView?id=xcloud.wallet_consumption_analytics_dash.htm&amp;language=en_US&amp;type=5" target="_blank" rel="noopener">Consumption Analytics Dashboard (Tableau Next)</a></p></td></tr><tr><td><p>Set customized threshold alerts to receive usage notifications when your orgâs usage exceeds specified thresholds. Also includes automated Monthly Account Summary emails that summarize usage from the previous month.</p></td><td><p><a href="https://help.salesforce.com/s/articleView?id=xcloud.wallet_get_notified.htm&amp;type=5" target="_blank" rel="noopener">Get Notifications about Your Usage</a></p></td></tr></tbody></table>

For more information about Digital Wallet access, see
[Access Digital Wallet](https://help.salesforce.com/s/articleView?id=xcloud.wallet_access.htm&type=5).

## See Also

- [Digital WalletâEnabled Consumption-Based Products](https://help.salesforce.com/s/articleView?id=xcloud.wallet_enabled_products_rate_cards.htm&type=5)
  (Salesforce Help)
- [Mule Credit Rate Card](https://www.salesforce.com/en-us/wp-content/uploads/sites/4/documents/mulesoft/Mule_Credit_Rate_Card.pdf)

# MuleSoft Automation Credits 1.0

> For the full documentation index, see: https://docs.mulesoft.com/llms.txt

Use MuleSoft Automation Credits 1.0 with Composer, IDP, RPA, or a combination of these products.
Consumption aggregates over the term of your subscription (not per session) and is then converted to
credits as shown below. The credits are deducted from your assigned quota for the subscription
period.

If you have MuleSoft Automation Credits 1.0, view usage visibility through Anypoint Usage Reports.
Sequoia handles monthly statements and overage billing.

<table><colgroup><col> <col></colgroup><thead><tr><th>Usage type</th><th>Conversion rate (Units per Credit)</th></tr></thead><tbody><tr><td><p>RPA Bot Minutes</p></td><td><p>2</p></td></tr><tr><td><p>RPA API Calls</p></td><td><p>100</p></td></tr><tr><td><p>Composer Tasks</p></td><td><p>50</p></td></tr><tr><td><p>IDP Document Pages</p></td><td><p>5</p></td></tr></tbody></table>

MuleSoft adds all the RPA execution times in seconds for the period and converts the total into
minutes with two decimal precision. MuleSoft reports Automation Credits with two-decimal precision.

For example, if you run 5 processes that take 30 seconds each to execute: MuleSoft converts the
total execution time (150 seconds) to minutes, rounds up the last two digits: 150/60 = 2.5 minutes,
and then converts the minutes to Automation Credits and rounds up the last two digits: 2.5/2 = 1.25
credits.

For detailed definitions and more information, see the
[https://www.mulesoft.com/prod-subscription-plans](https://www.mulesoft.com/prod-subscription-plans).

## See Also

- [Automation Credits Usage and Rates](automation-credits-usage-and-rates)
- [MuleSoft Product Subscription Plans](https://www.mulesoft.com/prod-subscription-plans)
- [Usage Reports](usage-reports)
---

# MuleSoft Automation Credits 2.0

> For the full documentation index, see: https://docs.mulesoft.com/llms.txt

Use MuleSoft Automation Credits 2.0 with Composer, IDP, RPA, Salesforce Flow Orchestration, MuleSoft
for Flow: Integration, or a combination of these products, as more fully described in the applicable
[Subscription Plan](https://www.salesforce.com/company/legal/mulesoft-subscription-plan/) or
Features and Entitlement Document.

If you have MuleSoft Automation Credits 2.0, view usage visibility through Anypoint Usage Reports.
Sequoia handles monthly statements and overage billing.

<table><colgroup><col> <col></colgroup><thead><tr><th>Usage type</th><th>Conversion rate (Credits per unit)</th></tr></thead><tbody><tr><td><p>RPA Bot Minutes</p></td><td><p>80</p></td></tr><tr><td><p>RPA API Calls</p></td><td><p>2</p></td></tr><tr><td><p>Composer Tasks</p></td><td><p>2</p></td></tr><tr><td><p>Flow Orchestration Runs</p></td><td><p>100</p></td></tr><tr><td><p>IDP Document Pages</p></td><td><p>30</p></td></tr><tr><td><p>Integration Tasks</p></td><td><p>2</p></td></tr></tbody></table>

To learn more about how Omni Credits are consumed, and when other types of credits are consumed with
generative AI features, see
[Agentforce and Generative AI Usage and Billing](https://help.salesforce.com/s/articleView?id=ai.generative_ai_usage.htm&type=5)
in Salesforce Help.

# MuleSoft Automation Credits 3.0

> For the full documentation index, see: https://docs.mulesoft.com/llms.txt

MuleSoft Automation Credits 3.0 is available in Digital Wallet. Use Automation Credits 3.0 with
MuleSoft for Flow: Integration, Anypoint IDP, MuleSoft for Flow: IDP, or a combination of these
products, as more fully described in the applicable
[Subscription Plan](https://www.salesforce.com/company/legal/mulesoft-subscription-plan/) or
Features and Entitlement Document.

If you have MuleSoft Automation Credits 3.0, usage visibility, monthly statements, and overage
billing are all centralized within the Salesforce Digital Wallet. If you have an Anypoint Platform
organization, view your usage and entitlement details within Anypoint Usage Reports for
cross-platform transparency.

> [!NOTE] Composer and RPA are moving to the end of sale. As of February 18, 2026, Flow
> Orchestration is included and does not consume Automation Credits in 3.0.

<table><colgroup><col> <col> <col></colgroup><thead><tr><th>Usage Type</th><th>Description</th><th>Conversion rate (Credits per unit)</th></tr></thead><tbody><tr><td><p><strong>MuleSoft for Flow: Integration</strong></p></td><td><p>Integration capabilities within Salesforce Flow; consumes Automation Credits for integration tasks.</p></td><td><p>2 credits per successful task</p></td></tr><tr><td><p><strong>IDP Document Pages (Anypoint Platform)</strong></p></td><td><p>IDP on Anypoint Platform; consumes credits for IDP document pages.</p></td><td><p>30 credits per page</p></td></tr><tr><td><p><strong>MuleSoft for Flow: IDP</strong></p></td><td><p>IDP capabilities within Salesforce Flow; consumes Automation Credits for document processing in Flow.</p></td><td><p>30 credits per page</p></td></tr></tbody></table>

Rates and usage types for Automation Credits 3.0 vary by subscription. Refer to the Usage Details
table on your Order Form and the applicable Subscription Plan or Features and Entitlement Document.

To learn more about how Omni Credits are consumed, and when other types of credits are consumed with
generative AI features, see
[Agentforce and Generative AI Usage and Billing](https://help.salesforce.com/s/articleView?id=ai.generative_ai_usage.htm&type=5)
in Salesforce Help.

# Upgrading Java for Custom Connectors (Partners)

> For the full documentation index, see: https://docs.mulesoft.com/llms.txt

Standard support for Java 8 and 11 ends in August 2026 for 4.6 LTS. Plan your upgrade path for apps
that are running on Java 8 or 11 accordingly.

> [!NOTE] This guide applies to MuleSoft partners only. If you are a MuleSoft customer, see
> [Upgrading Java for Custom Connectors (Customers)](customer-connector-upgrade).

Upgrade, test, and release your custom connectors for Java 17 to ensure compatibility within the
MuleSoft ecosystem. Ensuring this compatibility is crucial because running an incompatible connector
can lead to runtime failures, decreased performance, and security vulnerabilities that could impact
your appâs stability.

## Before You Begin

Before upgrading and testing your custom connector for Java 17, you must be familiar with the
following considerations:

- Target compilation level

  In Mule 4.9.0 and later, you can compile any Mule project for Java 17 or lower. Additional
  dependencies must be compiled for Java 17 or lower.

  If you compile for Java 17, upgrade the `mule-modules-parent` to version 1.9.0 or later. The
  connectorâs minimum Mule version will also be 4.9.0, ensuring compatibility only with Mule 4.9.0
  or later. Verify that `minMuleVersion` in `META-INF/mule-artifact/mule-artifact.json` is 4.9.0 or
  later. See
  [Specify Java Compatibility](../mule-sdk/latest/java-version-support#specify-java-compatibility).

  If the `mule-modules-parent` version is earlier than 1.9.0, the bytecode level for the connector
  and its additional dependencies must be for Java 8. You canât use language features from Java 11
  or Java 17.

- Unsupported directives

  Donât set `--add-opens`, `--add-exports`, or `--add-modules` directives. These directives are not
  allowed in CloudHub, Runtime Fabric, or on-premises. These directives also require managing them
  for each connector across all environments, and introduce a backward compatibility burden,
  complicating future Java upgrades and potentially enabling security vulnerabilities. If these
  directives are used, they are not covered under Support.

- Minimum Mule version

  If your custom connector is running on Mule 4.5.x or earlier, you canât run Java 17 tests that
  extend the `MuleArtifactFunctionalTestCase`, but you can still run Java 8 tests.

  To decouple the minimum Mule version when running tests, add the `-Dmunit.jvm` parameter to use
  Java 17 as described in
  [Test for Java 17 Compatibility Directly](#test-for-java-17-compatibility-directly).

## Upgrade Your Custom Mule SDK Connectors

Upgrade your custom Mule SDK (Java SDK and XML SDK) connectors to Java 17.

### Upgrade Third-Party Libraries

Your custom connector likely relies on third-party libraries, so itâs important to ensure they are
compatible with Java 17 as well. Even if your custom connector passes tests, it is still important
to deliver secure and reliable software. If a critical bug or vulnerability is found, you must
upgrade to a fixed version. Using up-to-date libraries that explicitly support Java 17 makes this
process easier and more reliable, as the vendor has already tested them on Java 17.

Upgrade the third-party libraries of your custom connector to be compatible with Java 17:

- Java EE libraries

  Mule 4.6.x exposes Java EE libraries differently than earlier Mule versions. You must add the BOM
  dependency to your custom connector and, if applicable, exclude the provided conflicting library.
  You can also add the libraries in the BOM dependency separately.

  ```java
  <properties>
   <muleJavaEeBomVersion>4.6.0</muleJavaEeBomVersion>
  </properties>
  <dependencyManagement>
   <dependencies>
     <dependency>
       <groupId>org.mule</groupId>
       <artifactId>mule-javaee-runtime-bom</artifactId>
       <version>${muleJavaEeBomVersion}</version>
       <type>pom</type>
       <scope>import</scope>
     </dependency>
   </dependencies>
  </dependencyManagement>
  ```

  For more information, see [Java EE Libraries](../mule-sdk/latest/java-ee-libraries).

- External libraries

  Upgrade external libraries, which are third-party libraries that are not bundled with your custom
  connector. Some examples include a JDBC driver, JMS broker client, or Groovy runtime. The
  following example shows a JDBC driver external library configuration:

  ```java
  @ExternalLib(name = "MySQL JDBC Driver",
  description = "A JDBC driver that supports connection to the MySQL Database",
  nameRegexpMatcher = "(.*)\\.jar",
  requiredClassName = "com.mysql.jdbc.Driver",
  coordinates = "mysql:mysql-connector-java:5.1.44")
  public class MySqlConnectionProvider implements ConnectionProvider<Connection> {
    //
  }
  ```

  You must update your external libraries to a version that is compatible with Java 17.

### Review Your Code

After you compile your custom connector, ensure you can run your custom connector with Java 17.
Reviewing your code is important because some restrictions are flagged as warnings in Java 11 but
are flagged as failures and canât be disabled in Java 17. For example, reflection is more restricted
in Java 17, so you must review your code to ensure there are no illegal actions.

To help you review your code, learn more about the different changes in Java 17:

- JDK Migration Guide

  See the
  [Oracle JDK Migration Guide](https://docs.oracle.com/en/java/javase/17/migrate/getting-started.html#GUID-C25E2B1D-6C24-4403-8540-CFEA875B994A)
  for a full list of the changes made from Java 8 to Java 17. Use the guide to assess the impact to
  your code.

- Mule runtime engine

  See [Mule runtime](java-support#mule-runtime) to learn more about the Java 17 changes made in Mule
  runtime engine.
  - Reflection on Java classes

    To ensure compatibility with Java 17, review and update any code that uses reflection to access
    Java classes or set parameter values. Java 17 imposes stricter access controls, so avoid
    introspecting JDK internals. Have a robust test suite that runs with Java 17. Pay special
    attention to the `setAccessible(true)` method because these access changes are not allowed in
    Java 17.

  - Serialization

    Update your serialization code to accommodate the restrictions of Java 17. If your code uses
    libraries like Gson for serializing classes, switch to using Data Transfer Objects (DTOs) where
    reflective access to JDK classes is involved. Ensure that serialization libraries are updated
    and adjust your code to minimize reliance on reflection. For more information, refer to
    [Configure Custom Serializers](../mule-runtime/latest/configure-custom-serializers).

  - Library upgrades

    Upgrade your libraries to versions compatible with Java 17. This includes updating ByteBuddy to
    version 1.14.0, Jacoco to 0.8.10, and SLF4J to version 2.x. Replace CGLib with ByteBuddy and
    check for updates to other libraries like Groovy and JRuby. Verify that all dependencies are
    compatible with Java 17 to avoid runtime issues.

  - Changes with running the test suite

    Adapt your testing approach for Java 17. If you use Mockito, be aware that Mockito canât mock
    JVM classes. Consider custom implementations for mocking and switch from PowerMock to newer
    Mockito versions. Update your tests to handle the changes in Java 17 and avoid deprecated
    methods.

  - Java Platform Module System (JPMS)

    JPMS was introduced in Java 9. This feature introduces stricter encapsulation, impacting how
    Mule modules interact with JDK classes. As Mule upgrades to Java 17 and JPMS, these changes are
    necessary:
    - Refactor Split Packages: Resolve issues with internal and API package splits by reorganizing
      and refactoring packages to conform with JPMS modularization standards.
    - Address `ResourceBundle` Loading: Implement solutions for issues related to resource bundle
      loading, such as using `ResourceBundleProvider` or alternative methods.
    - Analyze the third-party libraries' changelogs to understand Java 17 compatibility and changes
      related to JPMS.

### Add Missing Code

Because reflection is more restricted in Java 17, API objects now require setters. Previously with
Java 8, it was enough for API objects to only have default constructors and getters for all
DataWeave accessible properties. Now, API objects must also have setters so DataWeave can write
those properties without using reflection.

Constructors and setters are required if your class is instantiated by DataWeave, and getters are
required if your class is read. If your class is returned and not instantiated, only getters are
required. However, using both getters and setters simplifies the validation and certification
process.

## Upgrade Your Custom REST SDK Connectors

Use REST SDK version 0.8.0-RC4 to upgrade your custom REST SDK connectors to Java 17.

REST SDK 0.8.0-RC4 generates Java 17-compatible connectors and maintains backward compatibility only
with REST SDK 0.8.0-RC3 connectors built without Java customizations. Version 0.8.0-RC4 is not
backward-compatible with REST SDK versions earlier than 0.8.0-RC3.

When upgrading REST SDK connectors to Java 17:

- For REST SDK version 0.8.0-RC3 and earlier connectors with Java customizations, regenerate the
  connector with REST SDK 0.8.0-RC4 and port over any Java customizations.

  The resulting connector likely breaks compatibility with the previous version of the connector.

- For REST SDK 0.8.0-RC3 and earlier connectors without Java customizations, regenerate the
  connector with REST SDK 0.8.0-RC4.

  The resulting connector is backward-compatible with the previous version.

For more information about REST SDK, see
[REST SDK](https://beta.docs.mulesoft.com/beta-mule-sdk/mule-sdk/1.1/rest-sdk/rest-sdk-connectivity).

### Upgrade an Existing Custom REST SDK Connector

If you previously generated a connector using REST SDK and you want to make that connector
compatible with Java 17:

1.  Upgrade the REST SDK components and dependencies to version 0.8.0-RC4:

    ```xml
    <parent>
       <groupId>com.mulesoft.connectivity</groupId>
       <artifactId>rest-sdk-connector-parent-pom</artifactId>
       <version>0.8.0-RC4</version>
    </parent>

    <rest.sdk.commons.version>0.8.0-RC4</rest.sdk.commons.version>
    <rest.sdk.mojo.version>0.8.0-RC4</rest.sdk.mojo.version>
    ```

2.  If you manually create the configuration class (`ConnectorNameConfiguration.java`) using the
    REST SDK override feature, you must add an extra annotation:

    ```java
    import org.mule.sdk.api.annotation.JavaVersionSupport;
    import org.mule.sdk.api.meta.JavaVersion;

    @JavaVersionSupport({JavaVersion.JAVA_8, JavaVersion.JAVA_11, JavaVersion.JAVA_17})

    public class YourConnectorConfiguration
    ```

3.  Regenerate the connector.

### Upgrade a New Custom REST SDK Connector

If you generate a new connector using REST SDK and want to make that connector compatible with Java
17:

1.  Upgrade the REST SDK components and dependencies to version 0.8.0-RC4:

    ```xml
    <parent>
       <groupId>com.mulesoft.connectivity</groupId>
       <artifactId>rest-sdk-connector-parent-pom</artifactId>
       <version>0.8.0-RC4</version>
    </parent>

    <rest.sdk.commons.version>0.8.0-RC4</rest.sdk.commons.version>
    <rest.sdk.mojo.version>0.8.0-RC4</rest.sdk.mojo.version>
    ```

2.  Upgrade the Mule runtime version to 4.6.x.

## Upgrade Your Custom REST Connect Connectors

Upgrade your custom REST Connect connectors to Java 17.

REST Connect now supports Java 17. REST Connect connectors are generated from an API specification
using REST Connect. To make the connector Java 17-compatible, republish the API specification to
Exchange. See [REST Connect Connector Generator](../exchange/to-deploy-using-rest-connect).

> [!IMPORTANT] REST Connect now adds support for TLS. To save time, enable TLS at the same time you
> update your generated connector for Java 17 so that you need to generate the connector and test
> your apps only once.

## Upgrade Your Custom Configuration Properties Providers

To upgrade your custom configuration properties provider to be compatible with Java 17, switch to
use [Java SDK](../mule-sdk/latest/getting-started) and use the `@JavaVersionSupport` annotation as
explained in [Release Your Custom Connector](#release-your-custom-connector). You must also perform
all the steps described in
[Upgrading Java for Custom Connectors (Partners)](partner-connector-upgrade), just like with any
custom connector. The example custom configuration properties provider mentioned in
[Example: Mule SDK Module](../mule-runtime/latest/custom-configuration-properties-provider#example-mule-sdk-module)
is updated to support Java 17. Refer to that example to update your custom configuration properties
provider. For more details about what the changed code looks like (including migrating tests to
MUnit), refer to this
[changeset](https://github.com/mulesoft/mule-custom-properties-providers-module-example/commit/410ee26d8b3c44adacb077e099f32886f99eb6be).

Alternatively, if switching to using [Java SDK](../mule-sdk/latest/getting-started) involves too
many changes for you, add a declaration using `ExtensionDeclarer` inside `ExtensionLoadingDelegate`
in your custom configuration properties provider. For more details about what the changed code looks
like, refer to this
[changeset](https://github.com/mulesoft/mule-custom-properties-providers-module-example/commit/19d9d45cd52b0695f7b9c2b9019bae88f45fb228).

## Test Your Custom Connector with MTF

Test your custom connector with Module Testing Framework (MTF) to ensure Java 17 compatibility. For
more information about MTF, see [MTF](https://beta.docs.mulesoft.com/beta-mtf/mule-sdk/1.1/mtf).

### Set Up Your Build

Ensure your pipeline runs against all supported Java versions (Java 8, Java 11, and Java 17). The
following example shows a single build pipeline that is configured to run tests against all
supported Java versions, in which `default` corresponds to Java 17:

![Example of single build pipeline](_images/single-build-pipeline.png)

The pipeline runs all tests even if the previous tests fail. For example, the pipeline runs Java 17
tests even if the Java 11 tests fail.

Although the pipeline contains multiple tests, the pipeline has one compilation phase and one
release phase, which targets Java 8.

### Run an Initial Test

Run an initial test to test your custom connector for Java 17 compatibility. You can continue to run
tests as you change the custom connector code:

1.  In the `pom.xml` file of your custom connector, update the munit-extensions-maven-plugin
    configuration to include the following configuration (the `jacoco.version` property must be
    0.8.10 or later):

    ```xml
    <argLines>
             <argLine>                      -javaagent:${settings.localRepository}/org/jacoco/org.jacoco.agent/${jacoco.version}/org.jacoco.agent-${jacoco.version}-runtime.jar=destfile=${session.executionRootDirectory}/target/jacoco-munit.exec</argLine>
    </argLines>
    ```

2.  Run your MTF test to generate the coverage report. Use the `-Dtest=none` and
    `-DfailIfNoTests=false` flags when running the test to avoid including the JUnit tests in the
    coverage report.

    The coverage report is available in `target/jacoco-munit.exec`.

### View your Coverage Report

View your coverage report to see your custom connector coverage. You must have at least 80% coverage
for a high certainty of Java 17 compatibility.

1.  Open IntelliJ IDEA.
2.  Go to **Run** > **Show Coverage Data**.
3.  In **Choose Coverage Suite to Display**, add `jacoco-munit.exec` to the list if itâs not there
    already.
4.  Look at the coverage percentages to analyze your results.

The coverage percentage reflects how much of your connector is tested. A key issue to avoid is
illegal reflective access in your code. The only ways to detect this are to
[Review Your Code](#review-your-code) or through testing. Aiming for at least 80% coverage allows
flexibility on simpler code, such as getters and setters, while ensuring thorough testing of all
critical business logic.

### Add the JDeps Maven Plugin

JDeps is a tool for static code analysis that detects the usage of JDK internal APIs that are no
longer available or accessible. For more information, refer to the
[OpenJDK wiki](https://wiki.openjdk.org/spaces/JDK8/pages/17957128/Java+Dependency+Analysis+Tool).

Add the JDeps Maven plugin to your custom connectorâs `pom.xml` file:

```xml
<plugin>
    <groupId>org.apache.maven.plugins</groupId>
    <artifactId>maven-jdeps-plugin</artifactId>
    <version>3.1.2</version>
    <executions>
        <execution>
            <goals>
               <goal>jdkinternals</goal> <!-- verify main classes -->
               <goal>test-jdkinternals</goal> <!-- verify test classes -->
            </goals>
        </execution>
    </executions>
    <configuration>
        <failOnWarning>true</failOnWarning>
    </configuration>
</plugin>
```

If your custom connector is using a JDK internal API that is no longer available or accessible, the
build fails.

Run the build with both Java 8 and 11 by changing the `JAVA_HOME` value, as the JDeps Maven plugin
relies on the JDeps tool bundled with the JDK. Running the build with each Java version ensures
extra confidence.

To run the JDeps Maven plugin without tests, use the following command:

```bash
mvn clean install -Dtest=none -DfailIfNoTests=false -DskipTests=true
```

This helps focus on checking the offending libraries and internal access issues without dealing with
potential test failures or long test runtimes.

### Test for Java 17 Compatibility

You can test for Java 17 compatibility running on either Java 11 or Java 17.

If you are running on Java 11, you can perform early validations by adding a parameter for illegal
reflective access. See
[Add a Parameter for Illegal Reflective Access](#add-a-parameter-for-illegal-reflective-access).

If you are running on Java 17, you can test for Java 17 directly. See
[Test for Java 17 Compatibility Directly](#test-for-java-17-compatibility-directly).

#### Add a Parameter for Illegal Reflective Access

Reflective access is one of the breaking changes of Java 17. If you run your MTF tests with the
default Java 11 behavior, the MTF tests log only a warning for reflective access.

To resemble Java 17 behavior, run your MTF tests with the `--illegal-access=deny` JVM parameter so
the MTF tests fail instead of logging only a warning. Use this parameter in Mule runtime versions
4.2.0 and later.

To set up your custom connectorâs `pom.xml` file to include the configuration:

1.  Add an empty property:

    ```xml
    <mtf.javaopts></mtf.javaopts>
    ```

2.  Update the munit-extensions-maven-plugin configuration to include the following configuration:

    ```xml
    <environmentVariables>
       <!-- Toggles the JDK17 style flag -->
       <_JAVA_OPTIONS>-XX:+PrintCommandLineFlags ${mtf.javaopts}</_JAVA_OPTIONS>
    </environmentVariables>
    ```

You can now run your MTF tests with the `--illegal-access=deny` parameter. Here is an example bash
script (replace with the latest Mule runtime version available):

```bash
#!/bin/bash
RUNTIME_VERSION=4.6.0
MUNIT_JVM=/Library/Java/JavaVirtualMachines/adoptopenjdk-11.jdk/Contents/Home/bin/java
mvn clean
mkdir target
mvn verify \
    -DruntimeProduct=MULE_EE \
    -DruntimeVersion=$RUNTIME_VERSION \
    -Dmunit.jvm=$MUNIT_JVM \
    -Dmtf.javaopts="--illegal-access=deny" > ./target/test.log
```

After running your MTF tests, go to the `target/illegal-access.log` file and check for classes or
dependencies that misbehave.

You can also use the following command to exclude the known warnings outside of your custom
connector:

```bash
cat target/illegal-access.log | sort | uniq | grep -Ev "org.mule.module.artifact|org.mule.metadata|org.mule.runtime|org.mule.service"
```

#### Test for Java 17 Compatibility Directly

Run your MTF tests to test compatibility of your custom connector against Java 17.

As mentioned previously, you can use a single build pipeline that runs against all supported Java
versions. You can also set up another temporary build pipeline for Java 17 so your main build
pipeline doesnât become unstable. After you upgrade to Java 17, discard the temporary build pipeline
and converge on your main build pipeline.

1.  Set the path to your JVM installation in the `MUNIT_JVM` variable (you must install it
    yourself). You must also set `JAVA_HOME` to Java 8.
2.  Ensure the following MTF dependencies are set in your custom connector `pom.xml` file:
    - munit 3.1.0
    - munit-extensions-maven-plugin 1.2.0
    - mtf-tools 1.2.0
    - mule-maven-plugin 4.1.0
    - mule-extensions-maven-plugin 1.6.0-rc1

These MTF dependencies require a minimum Mule version of 4.3.0. To ensure your MTF tests donât
validate against Mule runtime versions earlier than 4.3.0, add the following to the `munit-plugin`
configuration in your custom connector `pom.xml` file:

```xml
<configuration>
	[...]
<runtimeConfiguration>
    <discoverRuntimes>
        <minMuleVersion>${minVersion}</minMuleVersion>
        <includeSnapshots>false</includeSnapshots>
        <product>EE</product>
    </discoverRuntimes>
</runtimeConfiguration>
</configuration>
```

You can run MTF tests against Java 17 only with Mule runtime 4.6.0 and later. For Mule runtime 4.5.x
and earlier, you can run MTF tests only against Java 8 and Java 11.

MUnit 3.1 is compatible only with Mule runtime 4.3.0 and later. If your connector is compatible with
Mule runtime 4.2.0 and earlier, you must create a legacy profile that overrides the MUnit version.

Use the following bash script to test your custom connector against Java 17:

```bash
#!/bin/bash
RUNTIME_VERSION=4.6.0
MUNIT_JVM=/Library/Java/JavaVirtualMachines/temurin-17.jdk/Contents/Home/bin/java
mvn clean
mkdir target
mvn verify \
   -DruntimeProduct=MULE_EE \
   -DruntimeVersion=$RUNTIME_VERSION \
   -Dmunit.jvm=$MUNIT_JVM \
   -Dmule.module.tweaking.validation.skip=true \
   -Dmule.jvm.version.extension.enforcement=LOOSE > ./target/test.log
```

The Mule runtime version you use determines the version of the mule-modules-parent. For example, if
you use Mule runtime 4.6.0, you must use mule-modules-parent 1.6.0. Minor versions maintain a
correspondence, such as Mule runtime 4.1.0 with mule-modules parent 1.1.0, Mule runtime 4.2.0 with
mule-modules-parent 1.2.0, and so forth.

Java 17 is supported with Mule runtime 4.6.0 and later. However, a connector can be compatible with
both Mule 4.3.0 and Java 17 simultaneously. If your connector must be compatible with Mule 4.3.0,
its mule-modules-parent version cannot exceed 1.3.0. You donât necessarily need to use
mule-modules-parent 1.6.0 for your connector to be compatible with Java 17. Using
mule-modules-parent 1.6.0 is specifically required to leverage other features from the Mule runtime
4.6.0 in the connector.

### Read Your Tests

After you run your MTF tests, your build has either of the following outcomes:

- Test failures

  You probably need to change your custom connector code to ensure Java 17 compatibility.

- All tests pass

  Either your custom connector does not require any major changes or your test suite is not
  comprehensive enough. Review your test suite and double-check that your code coverage is good and
  that your test scenarios and assertions are not too simple.

## Release Your Custom Connector

After you update your code and your tests are green, you are ready to release a new Java
17-compatible version of your custom connector.

1.  To communicate Java 17 compatibility, generate metadata for Java compatibility of your custom
    connector by adding or upgrading the custom connector `mule-sdk-api` dependency to the latest
    version:

    ```xml
    <dependency>
       <groupId>org.mule.sdk</groupId>
       <artifactId>mule-sdk-api</artifactId>
       <version>0.10.1</version>
    </dependency>
    ```

2.  For Java SDK, add the `@JavaVersionSupport` annotation in the same class as the `@Extension`
    annotation and include the `JAVA_17` value, for example:

    > [!NOTE] You donât need to add any annotations for XML SDK because XML SDK modules are Java 17
    > compatible and inherit the property automatically.

    ```java
    @Extension(name = "Database")
    @Operations(...)
    @JavaVersionSupport({JAVA_8, JAVA_11, JAVA_17})
    public class DatabaseConnector {
    ..
    }
    ```

In Mule 4.5.0 and later, custom connectors that donât specify the `@JavaVersionSupport` annotation
are assumed to be compatible with Java 8 and Java 11.

You can mark your custom connector as compatible with Java 17 only; however, you must ensure that no
adoption or backward compatibility issues exist.

When you deploy a Mule app, Mule verifies that all modules in the Mule app are compatible with the
Java version. If Mule finds an incompatibility, Mule throws an error and the application does not
deploy.

> [!NOTE] If you receive an error message specific to an XML SDK based connector, such as
> `Extension 'module-error-handler-plugin' does not support Java 17. Supported versions are: [1.8, 11]`,
> this means that your Mule app still contains some connectors that are not compatible with Java 17.
> To resolve this error, upgrade all connectors in your Mule app to be compatible with Java 17.

If your code is compatible with Java 17 but you donât declare Java 17 compatibility, you can still
get a successful test run.

To run a quick check on your custom connector or if all dependencies are not ready, pass the
following argument to skip hard checks on the Java support declaration:

```bash
-M-Dmule.jvm.version.extension.enforcement=LOOSE
```

For more information, see [Java Version Support](../mule-sdk/latest/java-version-support).

## See Also

- [Java Support](java-support)
---

# Anypoint Platform Pricing

> For the full documentation index, see: https://docs.mulesoft.com/llms.txt

The Usage Reports page supports pricing packages for
[integration](https://www.mulesoft.com/anypoint-pricing). When your organization opts in to a
usage-based pricing plan, such as the Anypoint Integration Advanced package, you must redeploy your
apps. After redeploying your apps, your usage reports are shown.

For CloudHub and CloudHub 2.0, apps are automatically redeployed during the monthly runtime patch
update cycle. For more information, see
[CloudHub Runtime Continuous Updates](../cloudhub/cloudhub-app-runtime-version-updates) and
[CloudHub 2.0 Operating System Patch Updates](../cloudhub-2/ch2-patch-updates).

For Runtime Fabric, you must manually redeploy apps to remain in compliance with your Anypoint
Platform contract.

## Metrics Definitions

Usage-based pricing packages and their usage reports are based on your organizationâs use of
allotted Mule components.

To understand the technical aspects of metering and pricing concepts within Anypoint Platform, see
[Usage and Pricing Metrics Reference](usage-metrics) documentation.

## Global Resource Pool Limits

Root organizations provisioned with usage-based pricing receive a shared global pool of CPU and
memory resources for CloudHub and CloudHub 2.0 deployments across all business groups and
environments in the organization. This pool of resources is large enough to consume all the
purchased flows and messages without interruption.

If an organization runs out of resources, actions that consume more resources, such as deploying new
applications or starting stopped applications are blocked until sufficient resources are available.
You can free up resources by stopping or deleting unused applications, or reducing the replica size
or resources that an existing application consumes.

When your organization approaches the resource limit on the Applications List UI, Application
Deployment UI, and Manage Application UI, you receive a warning. To avoid reaching limits, allocate
only the needed resources by using granular replica sizes in CloudHub and CloudHub 2.0.

Contact Support if you require an exception when you reach the resource limit.

> [!NOTE] This limit doesnât impact applications configured with
> [Configuring Horizontal Autoscaling (HPA) for CloudHub 2.0 Deployments](../cloudhub-2/ch2-configure-horizontal-autoscaling).
> You can continue to deploy applications if Horizontal Pod Autoscaling is enabled even if you reach
> the global resource limits.

## CloudHub 2.0 Application Deployment

For organizations provisioned with a usage-based pricing package, CloudHub 2.0 uses replica sizing
for application deployment. You can choose replica sizes based on the application profile or size
and display the equivalent in terms of legacy vCores. Replica sizes with the suffix `.mem` are
memory-intensive options with the same compute.

<table><colgroup><col> <col> <col> <col> <col></colgroup><thead><tr><th>Offering</th><th>Equivalent vCore Size</th><th>Total Memory</th><th>Heap Memory</th><th>Storage</th></tr></thead><tbody><tr><td><div><div><p>mule.nano</p></div></div></td><td><div><div><p>0.05</p></div></div></td><td><div><div><p>1 GB</p></div></div></td><td><div><div><p>0.5 GB</p></div></div></td><td><div><div><p>8 GB</p></div></div></td></tr><tr><td><div><div><p>mule.micro</p></div></div></td><td><div><div><p>0.1</p></div></div></td><td><div><div><p>1 GB</p></div></div></td><td><div><div><p>0.5 GB</p></div></div></td><td><div><div><p>8 GB</p></div></div></td></tr><tr><td><div><div><p>mule.micro.mem</p></div></div></td><td><div><div><p>0.1</p></div></div></td><td><div><div><p>2 GB</p></div></div></td><td><div><div><p>1 GB</p></div></div></td><td><div><div><p>8 GB</p></div></div></td></tr><tr><td><div><div><p>mule.small</p></div></div></td><td><div><div><p>0.2</p></div></div></td><td><div><div><p>2 GB</p></div></div></td><td><div><div><p>1 GB</p></div></div></td><td><div><div><p>8 GB</p></div></div></td></tr><tr><td><div><div><p>mule.medium</p></div></div></td><td><div><div><p>0.5</p></div></div></td><td><div><div><p>3 GB</p></div></div></td><td><div><div><p>1.5 GB</p></div></div></td><td><div><div><p>10 GB</p></div></div></td></tr><tr><td><div><div><p>mule.medium.mem</p></div></div></td><td><div><div><p>0.5</p></div></div></td><td><div><div><p>5 GB</p></div></div></td><td><div><div><p>2.5 GB</p></div></div></td><td><div><div><p>10 GB</p></div></div></td></tr><tr><td><div><div><p>mule.large</p></div></div></td><td><div><div><p>1</p></div></div></td><td><div><div><p>4 GB</p></div></div></td><td><div><div><p>2 GB</p></div></div></td><td><div><div><p>12 GB</p></div></div></td></tr><tr><td><div><div><p>mule.large.mem</p></div></div></td><td><div><div><p>1</p></div></div></td><td><div><div><p>8 GB</p></div></div></td><td><div><div><p>4 GB</p></div></div></td><td><div><div><p>12 GB</p></div></div></td></tr><tr><td><div><div><p>mule.xlarge</p></div></div></td><td><div><div><p>2</p></div></div></td><td><div><div><p>7 GB</p></div></div></td><td><div><div><p>3.5 GB</p></div></div></td><td><div><div><p>20 GB</p></div></div></td></tr><tr><td><div><div><p>mule.xlarge.mem</p></div></div></td><td><div><div><p>2</p></div></div></td><td><div><div><p>12 GB</p></div></div></td><td><div><div><p>6 GB</p></div></div></td><td><div><div><p>20 GB</p></div></div></td></tr><tr><td><div><div><p>mule.2xlarge</p></div></div></td><td><div><div><p>4</p></div></div></td><td><div><div><p>15 GB</p></div></div></td><td><div><div><p>7.5 GB</p></div></div></td><td><div><div><p>20 GB</p></div></div></td></tr></tbody></table>

During the monthly patching cycle, your applications automatically migrate to an equivalent replica
size.

## Access Management Entitlements

Accounts provisioned with a usage-based pricing package show flows, messages, and throughput
entitlements in Access Management instead of vCores. These entitlements are available only at the
root organization level. Because vCores are no longer the purchased entity, they can no longer be
tracked or allocated among business groups. To view your monthly usage of flows, messages, and
throughput, [access usage reports](usage-reports#access-usage-reports) in the Access Management UI.

## See Also

- [Usage Reports](usage-reports)
---

# Anypoint Platform Security

> For the full documentation index, see: https://docs.mulesoft.com/llms.txt

Use Anypoint Platform to build security into your application network:

- Manage access for users, groups, environments, client providers, identity providers, connected
  apps, and external access with [the Access Management user interface](../access-management/). You
  can also manage access programmatically with
  [Access Management APIs](https://anypoint.mulesoft.com/exchange/portals/anypoint-platform/f1e97bc6-315a-4490-82a7-23abe036327a.anypoint-platform/access-management-api/).
- Apply policies to your APIs with
  [the API Manager user interface](../api-manager/latest/latest-overview-concept).
- Monitor Anypoint Platform for denial-of-service attacks and other security issues with
  [the Anypoint Monitoring user interface](../monitoring/).
---

# Troubleshooting Usage Reports

> For the full documentation index, see: https://docs.mulesoft.com/llms.txt

Because platform data availability depends on activity in your organization, in some cases, usage
reports are unavailable.

## Unable to Access Usage Reports in Anypoint Platform

Verify that you have the Usage Viewer permission assigned at the root organization level.

If you donât have the Usage Viewer permission assigned to you, contact the Anypoint Platform
organization administrator.

## A Supported Product Isnât listed

If a supported product isnât listed when you open the product list, click **Switch View**.

For more information about product availability by view, see
[available products](usage-reports#products-usage-reports).

## Usage Reports Donât Display Data for Runtime Fabric Apps

If you donât see any data for Runtime Fabric apps, ensure that:

- Youâre using Runtime Fabric agent version 1.13.112, 2.1.30, or later.
- After updating the Runtime Fabric agent, you manually redeployed each Runtime Fabric app with a
  supported date-patch version.

If youâre still missing Runtime Fabric apps in your usage reports, contact your account executive to
request a report of apps that are not upgraded and what actions you need to take to update them.

## A Month or a Day is Unavailable in the Date Picker

If the date picker doesnât show a month or day, data is unavailable for the selected period.

## Report Says No Data

If you select a product and daily or monthly report in new usage reports, and the report returns a
`No Data` message, no data is available for the selected period.

This means that there is no data available for the selected day or month.

## No Report Available for This Time Period Message

If, after selecting a month in legacy usage reports, the report returns
`No report available for this time period`, no data is available for the selected month.

This means there is no data available for the selected month.

## Deployed Mule App Isnât Listed in the Mule Runtime Usage Report

If a deployed Mule app isnât listed in the Mule Runtime usage report, make sure youâre using a
supported version of Mule runtime. For more information, see
[supported runtime versions](usage-reports#supported-mule-runtime-versions).

Redeploy your Mule apps with a supported Mule runtime version, either manually or during a monthly
patching cycle.

## See Also

- [Usage and Pricing Metrics Reference](usage-metrics)
- [Usage Reports](usage-reports)
- [Usage Viewer permission](../access-management/permissions-by-product#usage)
---

# Upgrading Java for Policies and API Proxies

> For the full documentation index, see: https://docs.mulesoft.com/llms.txt

Standard support for Java 8 and 11 ends in August 2026 for 4.6 LTS. Plan your upgrade path for apps
that are running on Java 8 or 11 accordingly.

Upgrade your policies and API proxies to be compatible with Java 17.

## Upgrading Automated Policies

Upgrade your automated policies (included and custom) to Java 17.

### Upgrading Included Automated Policies

To upgrade automated [MuleSoft included policies](../mule-gateway/policies-included-directory) to
Java 17:

1.  In API Manager, go to **Automated Policies**.
2.  In **Policy version**, select the latest version of the included policy.
3.  In **Rule of application**:
    - If your policy is on the latest version:
      - To apply included policies to all Mule runtimes, select **All runtimes** and click
        **Apply**.
      - To apply the included policies to Omni Gateways only, select **Omni gateways only** and
        click **Apply**.
      - To apply the included policies to Mule Gateways only:
        1.  Select **Mule gateways only**.
        2.  In **From version** and **To**, enter the Mule version range.
        3.  In **Java Versions**, select **All Java versions** or **Choose specific Java versions**.
        4.  Click **Apply**.

    - If your policy is not on the latest version:
      - To apply included policies to all Mule runtimes, select **All runtimes** and click
        **Apply**.
      - To apply the included policies to Omni Gateways only, select **Omni gateways only** and
        click **Apply**. Note that the **Rule of application** section must be the same as the
        **Rule of application** section in the latest version or else you might have unprotected
        APIs.
      - To apply the included policies to Mule Gateways only:
        1.  Select **Mule gateways only**.
        2.  In **From version** and **To**, enter the Mule version range.
        3.  In **Java versions**, select **Choose specific Java versions** and select Java 8 and
            Java 11.
        4.  Click **Apply**.
        5.  Click **Add automated policy** and add the same automated policy.
        6.  Click **Advanced options**.
        7.  Select **Mule gateways only**.
        8.  In **From version** and **To**, enter the Mule version range.
        9.  In **Java versions**, select **Choose specific Java versions** and select Java 17.
        10. Click **Apply**.

### Upgrading Custom Automated Policies

To upgrade custom automated policies to Java 17:

1.  In API Manager, go to **Automated Policies**.
2.  Upgrade the required extensions and connectors to Java 17.
    - If your custom policy has Anypoint Connectors, see
      [Upgrading Anypoint Connectors and Modules](java-support#anypoint-connectors-and-modules).
    - If your custom policy has custom connectors, see
      [Upgrading Custom Connectors](java-support#custom-connectors).

3.  [Upgrade the custom policy](../mule-gateway/policies-custom-java-version).
4.  [Package the custom policy](../mule-gateway/policies-custom-package).

    Configure Java 17 locally to make custom policies compatible with Java 17.

5.  [Republish the custom policy to Exchange](../mule-gateway/policies-custom-upload-to-exchange).
6.  Apply the new version of the custom policy that is compatible with Java 17 to the API instance:
    1.  Go to **Policies**.
    2.  In **Applied policy status**, verify that the latest definition is applied.
        - If the latest definition is applied, click on the More (â¦â) menu and select **Check for
          implementation updates**.
        - If the latest definition is not applied, update to the latest definition.

## Upgrading API-Level Policies

Upgrade your API-level policies (included and custom) to Java 17.

### Upgrading Included API-Level Policies

To upgrade API-level [MuleSoft included policies](../mule-gateway/policies-included-directory) to
Java 17:

1.  In API Manager, go to **API Administration** > **{API Name}** > **Policies** > **Add a policy**
    to [apply policies](../mule-gateway/policies-included-apply) at the individual API level.
2.  Upgrade each of your API-level policies:
    1.  In **Policy version**, select the latest version of the included policy.
    2.  In **Method & resource conditions**, select either **Apply configuration to all API method &
        resources** or **Apply configuration to specific API method & resources**.
    3.  Click **Apply**.

### Upgrading Custom API-level Policies

To upgrade custom API-level policies to Java 17:

1.  In API Manager, go to **API Administration** > **{API Name}** > **Policies** > **Add a policy**
    to [apply policies](../mule-gateway/policies-included-apply) at the individual API level.
2.  Upgrade the required extensions and connectors to Java 17.
    - If your custom policy has Anypoint Connectors, see
      [Upgrading Anypoint Connectors and Modules](java-support#anypoint-connectors-and-modules).
    - If your custom policy has custom connectors, see
      [Upgrading Custom Connectors](java-support#custom-connectors).

3.  [Upgrade the custom policy](../mule-gateway/policies-custom-java-version).
4.  [Package the custom policy](../mule-gateway/policies-custom-package).

    Configure Java 17 locally to make custom policies compatible with Java 17.

5.  [Republish the custom policy to Exchange](../mule-gateway/policies-custom-upload-to-exchange).
6.  Apply the new version of the custom policy that is compatible with Java 17 to the API instance:
    1.  Go to **Policies**.
    2.  In **Applied policy status**, verify that the latest definition is applied.
        - If the latest definition is applied, click on the More (â¦â) menu and select **Check for
          implementation updates**.
        - If the latest definition is not applied, update to the latest definition.

## Upgrading API Proxies

> [!IMPORTANT] To ensure your API proxies or Mule apps are protected when upgrading, upgrade your
> API policies **before** upgrading your API proxies or Mule apps.

The steps to upgrade are a little different, depending on your **Proxy type**:

- Basic endpoint:
  - If you use Basic endpoint, deploy the adapted application to the server from Mule runtime and
    connect it to API Manager using autodiscovery. For more information, see
    [Configuring Mule Gateway API Autodiscovery in a Mule 4 Application](../mule-gateway/mule-gateway-config-autodiscovery-mule4).
  - If you use a Basic endpoint API instance to update your instance, update the Mule application
    connecting to your API instance.

- If you use a proxy application, see the following sections for deployment type:
  - [CloudHub and CloudHub 2.0 Deployments](#cloudhub-and-cloudhub2-deployments)
  - [Hybrid Deployments](#hybrid-deployments)
  - [Runtime Fabric Deployments](#runtime-fabric-deployments)

### CloudHub and CloudHub 2.0 Deployments

Before following these steps, ensure your policies are updated. For more information, see
[Upgrading Automated Policies](#upgrading-automated-policies) and
[Upgrading API-Level Policies](#upgrading-api-level-policies).

1.  In API Manager, go to **API Administration > Settings**.
2.  [Edit the API instanceâs configuration settings](../api-manager/latest/edit-api-endpoint-task):
    1.  In **Runtime Channel**, select the runtime channel to use.
    2.  In **Version**, select **4.6.0** or later.
    3.  In **Java version**, select **Java 17**.

3.  Click **Save & Apply**.

### Hybrid Deployments

Before following these steps, ensure your policies are updated. For more information, see
[Upgrading Automated Policies](#upgrading-automated-policies) and
[Upgrading API-Level Policies](#upgrading-api-level-policies).

1.  Deploy Mule runtime engine 4.6.x in a new server running on Java 17 and start the server. For
    more information, see [Add Servers to Runtime Manager](../runtime-manager/servers-create).
2.  Select the new target you created running on Java 17 and, in API Manager, go to **API
    Administration > Settings**.
3.  [Edit the API instanceâs configuration settings](../api-manager/latest/edit-api-endpoint-task):
    1.  In **Select target**, select the server running on Java 17 as the target.

4.  Click **Save & Apply**.
5.  After the server successfully runs on Java 17, shift your traffic gradually to the new server
    using a load balancer and turn off the old server after all of its apps are migrated.

### Runtime Fabric Deployments

Before following these steps, ensure your policies are updated. For more information, see
[Upgrading Automated Policies](#upgrading-automated-policies) and
[Upgrading API-Level Policies](#upgrading-api-level-policies).

1.  Deploy Mule runtime engine 4.6.x in a new server running on Java 17 and start the server. For
    more information, see
    [Deploying API Proxies to Runtime Fabric](../runtime-fabric/latest/proxy-deploy-runtime-fabric).
2.  Select the new target you created running on Java 17 and, in API Manager, go to **API
    Administration > Settings**.
3.  [Edit the API instanceâs configuration settings](../api-manager/latest/edit-api-endpoint-task):
    1.  In **Runtime Channel**, select the runtime channel to use.
    2.  In **Version**, select **4.6.0** or later.
    3.  In **Java version**, select **Java 17**.

4.  Click **Save & Apply**.
5.  After the server successfully runs on Java 17, shift your traffic gradually to the new server
    using a load balancer and turn off the old server after all of its apps are migrated.

## See Also

- [Java Support](java-support)
---

# Usage and Pricing Metrics Reference

> For the full documentation index, see: https://docs.mulesoft.com/llms.txt

Anypoint Platform captures usage metrics across products, but not all metrics are billable or
reflected in usage reports.

<table><colgroup><col> <col> <col></colgroup><thead><tr><th>Product</th><th>Metric Description</th><th>Usage Details</th></tr></thead><tbody><tr><td rowspan="6"><p>Mule Runtime</p></td><td><p>Mule flow: Flow within a deployed and running Mule app that contains a Mule event source or route APIKit request</p></td><td><p>Flows are aggregated using a Max Concurrent model. The usage for a month is the highest number of flows that exist in a single given hour during a month.</p></td></tr><tr><td><p>Mule message: Container of the core information processed by the runtime</p></td><td><p>A Mule message counts as a single unit when an event source triggers it. Messages are aggregated using a total of all messages sent during a month.</p></td></tr><tr><td><p>Data throughput: Total amount of data transferred in and out of the infrastructure that runs Mule where the Mule app is deployed</p></td><td><p>Data throughput counts when the deployed application transfers data to execute its business logic, including but not limited to internal operational network traffic for monitoring, logs, and health checks. Data throughput is aggregated as a sum of all bytes during a month.</p></td></tr><tr><td><p>Cluster capacity: A set of workers or nodes that act as a single deployment target for a given Runtime Fabric instance</p></td><td><p>Allocatable CPU capacity of each node within the Runtime Fabric instance.</p></td></tr><tr><td><p>CPU Limit (Millicores): Maximum amount of CPU resources a worker node in Runtime Fabric can use</p></td><td><div><div><p>The amount of CPU usage is aggregated over a specific period of time, such as an hour or a day.</p></div><div><p>The CPU limit configuration of each application is summarized at each environment ID, then at each business group, and then at the root organization ID for preproduction (sandbox) and production environment types separately.</p></div></div></td></tr><tr><td><p>CPU Reserve (Millicores): A guaranteed minimum amount of CPU resources allocated to a worker node in the Runtime Fabric instance</p></td><td><p>CPU reserve is aggregated by calculating the total amount of CPU resources allocated by the user to reserve for applications within the cluster or Runtime Fabric instance.</p></td></tr><tr><td><p>API Manager</p></td><td><p>API instances: API instances in production, preproduction, and unclassified APIs (not associated with an environment) that are managed by API Manager after they are created using add, promote, or import options.</p></td><td><div><div><p>API instances remain under management until they are deleted. Instances of API Manager are aggregated using a Max Concurrent model with three separate metrics for production, preproduction, and unclassified (APIs that arenât associated with an environment).</p></div><div><p>+</p></div><div><div data-type="NOTE">Data for API Manager is available starting in October 2024.</div></div></div></td></tr><tr><td><p>API Governance</p></td><td><p>API under Governance: APIs identified by the selection criteria of at least one of the governance profiles</p></td><td><div><div><p>If an API is governed, all versions of that API are considered one governed API. Instances of API Governance are aggregated using a Max Concurrent model. The usage for a month is the highest number of APIs governed in a single given hour during a month.</p></div></div></td></tr><tr><td><p>Omni Gateway</p></td><td><p>Omni Gateway API call: Any access request received by Omni Gateway regardless of whether the response to the request is successful</p></td><td><p>Omni Gateway requests are aggregated as a total of all requests during a month.</p></td></tr><tr><td rowspan="3"><p>LLM Proxy</p></td><td><p>Prompt tokens: Number of tokens an LLM Proxy in the prompts an LLM Proxy sends to a specific LLM Model</p></td><td><p>These tokens are the sum of all prompt tokens a specific LLM Proxy sends to a specific LLM Model during a month. A prompt sent to an LLM Proxy are considered an Omni Gateway API call and count towards the Omni Gateway API call metric.</p></td></tr><tr><td><p>Completion tokens: Number of tokens in the responses that an LLM Proxy receives from a specific LLM Model</p></td><td><p>These tokens are the sum of all response tokens a specific LLM Proxy receives from a specific LLM Model during a month.</p></td></tr><tr><td><p>Total tokens: Total number of tokens used in interactions between an LLM Proxy and a specific LLM Model</p></td><td><p>Some LLM Providers, such as Gemini, use thinking tokens. Total tokens are the sum of prompt tokens, completion tokens, and thinking tokens if applicable.</p></td></tr><tr><td><p>Composer</p></td><td><p>Composer task: Any action executed on a Composer connector, including but not limited to read, create, update, and delete</p></td><td><p>Composer tasks are aggregated as a total of all actions during a month.</p></td></tr><tr><td><p>RPA</p></td><td><p>Robotic Process Automation (âRPAâ) bot minutes: The number of minutes running process automations across all bot sessions</p></td><td><p>A single bot can run multiple parallel sessions, with RPA bot minutes counting for each parallel session. You can configure multiple bots to run the same process, with RPA bot minutes counting for each of these separate bot sessions. Test runs or process runs in the test phase are also counted towards RPA bot minutes.</p></td></tr><tr><td><p>Anypoint MQ</p></td><td><p>Anypoint MQ API request: A request made to retrieve one or more messages from the Anypoint MQ APIs</p></td><td><p>Anypoint MQ uses API requests to calculate billing. Anypoint MQ API requests are calculated in the aggregate across all environments (including production, pre-production, sandbox, and design). Anypoint MQ API requests are available via API and aggregated on usage reports.</p></td></tr><tr><td><p>Object Store</p></td><td><p>Object Store API request: A request made to retrieve one or more messages from the Object Store APIs as further defined in the Object Store documentation</p></td><td><p>Each Object Store API request includes up to 100 KB of data. Object Store API requests over 100 KB count as multiple requests with no fractional units. Object Store API requests are available via API and aggregated on usage reports.</p></td></tr><tr><td><p>DataGraph</p></td><td><p>DataGraph orchestration: An API request made by Anypoint DataGraph to the source APIs to get data for the GraphQL API request made to Anypoint DataGraph</p></td><td><p>Orchestrations are not currently aggregated on usage reports.</p></td></tr><tr><td><p>Intelligent Document Processing (IDP)</p></td><td><p>Intelligent Document Processing (IDP) Document Pages: A single page processed by IDP</p></td><td><p>IDP document actions might process documents that have more than one page, with each page counting separately. When RPA executes document actions, it also counts towards document pages and additionally consumes the corresponding RPA Bot Minutes, accounting for the time the RPA process runs.</p></td></tr></tbody></table>

## Anypoint MQ Usage

Anypoint MQ usage reports show this information in tables and cards:

Business Group

Business group that owns the resources where the app with the queue is deployed

Environment

Environment within the business group where the app with the queue is deployed

Region id

Region where the app with the queue is deployed

Object type

Queue or message exchange

Object name

Name of the queue or message exchange

Message units

Number of encoded characters in a message, used to determine the size of message data in the
response for rate limiting (not billing)

Messages bytes

Size of the message data in the response, based on message units

API requests

Number of API calls via the connector or API, used to calculate monthly quota for billing

Messages received

Number of messages received

Total Message units

Sum of message units across all queues in the organization

Total Messages bytes

Sum of message data across all queues in the organization

Total API requests

Sum of API calls across all queues in the organization

Total Messages received

Sum of messages received across all queues in the organization

Sideloaded apps for hybrid standalone deployments are shown with the â icon in usage reports.
Redeployments of sideloaded apps result in multiple records appearing in usage reports.

## API Experience Hub Usage

API Experience Hub usage reports show this information in tables and cards:

Salesforce Organization ID

OrgID for the Salesforce organization where the API is deployed

Salesforce Portal Name

Name of the Salesforce portal where the API is deployed

\# of Approved Request Access

Sum of approved access requests for the API

Total # of Approved Request Access

Sum of approved access requests across all Salesforce organizations

## API Governance Usage

API Governance usage reports show this information in tables and cards:

Business Group

Business group that contains governed APIs

\# of APIs Governed

Sum of APIs governed in the specified business group

Maximum Number of Governed APIs

Highest number of APIs governed in a single given hour during a month

## API Manager Usage

Data for API Manager is available starting in October 2024.

API Manager usage reports show this information in tables and cards:

Business Group

Business group where the API is managed

Environment

Environment type where the API is managed

Runtime

Runtime type for the API

\# of APIs Managed

Sum of APIs managed in the specified business group, environment, and runtime

Maximum Number of Managed APIs

API instances in production, preproduction, and unclassified APIs (not associated with an
environment)

## Omni Gateway Usage

Omni Gateway usage reports show this information in tables and cards:

Business Group

Business group in which the Omni Gateway is registered.

Gateway Type

Gateway type of the Omni Gateway registered.

Data for this metric is available starting in March 2025.

Environment

Environment in which the Omni Gateway is registered.

Registration

Name of the registered Omni Gateway.

Data Egress (GB)

Total amount of outbound data in gigabytes (GB) transmitted by the Omni Gateway egress to upstream
services.

Data for this metric is available starting in March 2025.

\# of API Calls

Sum of API calls made by APIs that are deployed within the Omni Gateway.

Total # of API Calls

Sum of API calls across all registered Omni Gateways in the organization.

## LLM Proxy Usage

LLM Proxy usage reports show this information in tables and cards:

LLM Proxy

Name of the LLM Proxy

Business Group

Business group where the LLM Proxy is deployed

Client app

The client application or integration that sent requests to the LLM Proxy

Provider

The external LLM provider used by the proxy

Model

The specific LLM models used by the proxy

Prompt tokens

The sum of tokens in prompts the LLM Proxy sends to the model in the given time period

Completion tokens

The sum of tokens in responses the LLM Proxy receives from the model in the given time period

Total tokens

Total tokens used in interactions between the LLM Proxy and the model in the given time period. This
includes prompt tokens, completion tokens, and any provider-specific thinking tokens when
applicable.

## Intelligent Document Processing (IDP)

IDP usage reports show this information in tables and cards:

Business Group

Business group the document is processed in

Action ID

ID associated with the processed action

Action Version

Version associated with the processed action

Execution Type

Execution types associated with the processed action

Processed Pages

Total pages processed

## Mule Runtime Usage

MuleSoft captures usage data for Mule flows, Mule messages, and data throughput, but not all of this
data is aggregated in usage reports.
