To track Mule message usage, the runtime report counts the number of times a Mule event source
triggers a Mule message. You can view the number of these messages in a given day or month by
business group, environment, and application.

To calculate usage, MuleSoft meters and aggregates the number of messages daily and monthly. After a
message is triggered, the report doesnât track changes to the message because the message is
processed within the applicationâs flows.

Data throughput is the total network I/O bytes produced by the infrastructure of the Mule runtime
engine running the Mule application.

To calculate usage, MuleSoft tracks usage and aggregates the total daily and monthly GBs.

For some customers in the US control plane, MuleSoft offers a pricing and packaging model for
Anypoint Platform that allots a number of Mule flows, Mule messages, and data throughput (measured
as network I/O bytes). Not all types of Mule flows and messages count toward the allotments in a
package.

### Mule Runtime Usage Tables

The usage information shown in the usage report tables changes depending on your view.

#### Business Group Details

Select the **Business Group Details** tab to view:

<table><colgroup><col> <col></colgroup><thead><tr><th>Field</th><th>Description</th></tr></thead><tbody><tr><td><p>Business Group</p></td><td><p>Business group that owns the resources. For Runtime Fabric, itâs the business group used when creating the Runtime Fabric instance.</p></td></tr><tr><td><p>Environment Type</p></td><td><div><div><p>Environment the resources are associated with.</p></div><div><p>For Runtime Fabric, itâs the environment the Runtime Fabric instance is associated with. If any production environment is associated with a Runtime Fabric instance, it is considered a production instance. Cluster capacity is not split within a Runtime Fabric instance between preproduction (sandbox) and production environments.</p></div></div></td></tr><tr><td><p>Cluster</p></td><td><p>Name of the cluster or Runtime Fabric instance containing the applications, workers, and nodes.</p></td></tr><tr><td><p>Cluster Capacity (Millicores)</p></td><td><p>Allocatable CPU capacity of each node within the Runtime Fabric instance.</p></td></tr></tbody></table>

#### Application Details

Select the **Application Details** tab to view:

<table><colgroup><col> <col></colgroup><thead><tr><th>Field</th><th>Description</th></tr></thead><tbody><tr><td><p>Application</p></td><td><p>Name of the Mule application</p></td></tr><tr><td><p>Business Group</p></td><td><p>Business group that owns the resources. For Runtime Fabric, itâs the business group used when creating the Runtime Fabric instance.</p></td></tr><tr><td><p>Deployment Type</p></td><td><p>Runtime plane the Mule app is deployed to: CloudHub (abbreviated as CH), CloudHub 2.0 (abbreviated as CH2), or Runtime Fabric (abbreviated as RTF)</p></td></tr><tr><td><p>Environment Type</p></td><td><p>Preproduction (sandbox) or production environment the Mule app is deployed to.</p></td></tr><tr><td><p>Cluster</p></td><td><p>The Runtime Fabric instance that contains the nodes and applications</p></td></tr><tr><td><p>CPU Limit (Millicores)</p></td><td><p>Maximum amount of CPU allocated by the user that the application can burst to within the shared cluster or Runtime Fabric instance</p></td></tr><tr><td><p>CPU Reserve (Millicores)</p></td><td><p>Amount of CPU allocated by the user to reserve for applications within the Runtime Fabric cluster or instance</p></td></tr><tr><td><p>Mule Flows</p></td><td><p>Total number of flows in the Mule app, calculated by multiplying flows by the number of workers (CloudHub) or replicas (CloudHub 2.0 and Runtime Fabric)</p></td></tr><tr><td><p>Mule Messages</p></td><td><p>Total number of inbound and outbound Mule messages in the Mule app</p></td></tr><tr><td><p>Data Throughput (GB)</p></td><td><p>Total amount of inbound and outbound data in gigabytes (GB) transmitted by the Mule app</p></td></tr></tbody></table>

### Mule Runtime Usage Cards

MuleSoft usage reports cards include:

- [Maximum Mule Flows](#max-mule-flows)
- [Total Mule Messages](#mule-messages)
- [Maximum CPU Limit in Production](#max-cpu-prod)
- [Maximum CPU Limit in Preproduction](#max-cpu-preprod)
- [Maximum Cluster Capacity in Production](#max-cluster-prod)
- [Maximum Cluster Capacity in Preproduction](#max-cluster-preprod)

### Maximum Mule Flows

A Mule flow is a sequence of logical operations configured within the XML `<flow/>` element of a
Mule application. The runtime report tracks the Mule flows within a deployed and running Mule
application that contains a Mule event source or APIkit route requests.

Mule apps in production environments typically use multiple Mule flows and subflows to divide the
app into functional modules or for error-handling purposes. For example, one Mule flow might receive
a record and transform data into a given format that another flow processes.

To calculate usage, MuleSoft tracks the number of Mule flows for all business groups, environments,
and applications. The maximum number of Mule flows within a day or month is identified based on the
peak-hour usage across the day or month. For the detailed breakdown, MuleSoft shows the peak hour
usage per business group, environment, and application.

In a usage report, flow counts are calculated by multiplying the number of flows in an app by the
number of workers (CloudHub) or replicas (CloudHub 2.0 and Runtime Fabric).

#### Mule Flow Scenarios That Count Toward Your Anypoint Platform Package Allotment

These Mule flows count toward your allotment:

- Flows that contain a [Mule event source](../mule-runtime/latest/about-mule-event)
- [Flows generated by APIkit](../apikit/latest/) that process API requests

Mule flows are charged only when the application containing the Mule flow is deployed and running.

#### Mule Flow with an Event Source

This Mule flow contains an event source as the first element. In this case, the `listener` counts
towards your allotment.

```xml
<flow name="test-flow" >
        <http:listener config-ref="cocheras-puerto-madero-api-httpListenerConfig" path="/daily-report"/>
         <logger level="INFO" message="#[output json --- attributes.queryParams]" />
</flow>
```

#### Examples of Event Sources

<table><colgroup><col> <col></colgroup><thead><tr><th>Connector</th><th>Source</th></tr></thead><tbody><tr><td><p>aggregators</p></td><td><p>aggregator-listener</p></td></tr><tr><td><p>amqp</p></td><td><p>listener</p></td></tr><tr><td rowspan="2"><p>apikit-odata</p></td><td><p>request-entity-collection-listener</p></td></tr><tr><td><p>request-entity-listener</p></td></tr><tr><td rowspan="3"><p>as2-mule4</p></td><td><p>as2-listener</p></td></tr><tr><td><p>as2-mdn-listener</p></td></tr><tr><td><p>non-repudiation-listener</p></td></tr><tr><td><p>azure-service-bus-messaging</p></td><td><p>message-listener</p></td></tr><tr><td><p>core</p></td><td><p>scheduler</p></td></tr><tr><td><p>db</p></td><td><p>listener</p></td></tr><tr><td rowspan="2"><p>email</p></td><td><p>listener-imap</p></td></tr><tr><td><p>listener-pop3</p></td></tr><tr><td><p>file</p></td><td><p>listener</p></td></tr><tr><td><p>ftp</p></td><td><p>listener</p></td></tr><tr><td><p>ftps</p></td><td><p>listener</p></td></tr><tr><td rowspan="3"><p>google-sheets</p></td><td><p>new-row-listener</p></td></tr><tr><td><p>new-spreadsheet-listener</p></td></tr><tr><td><p>updated-row-listener</p></td></tr><tr><td><p>http</p></td><td><p>listener</p></td></tr><tr><td><p>ibm-mq</p></td><td><p>listener</p></td></tr><tr><td><p>jms</p></td><td><p>listener</p></td></tr><tr><td rowspan="2"><p>kafka</p></td><td><p>batch-message-listener</p></td></tr><tr><td><p>message-listener</p></td></tr><tr><td><p>mllp</p></td><td><p>mllp-listener</p></td></tr><tr><td rowspan="4"><p>netsuite</p></td><td><p>deleted-object-listener</p></td></tr><tr><td><p>modified-object-listener</p></td></tr><tr><td><p>modified-record-listener</p></td></tr><tr><td><p>new-record-listener</p></td></tr><tr><td><p>pubsub</p></td><td><p>message-listener</p></td></tr><tr><td rowspan="7"><p>salesforce</p></td><td><p>deleted-object-listener</p></td></tr><tr><td><p>modified-object-listener</p></td></tr><tr><td><p>new-object-listener</p></td></tr><tr><td><p>replay-channel-listener</p></td></tr><tr><td><p>replay-topic-listener</p></td></tr><tr><td><p>subscribe-channel-listener</p></td></tr><tr><td><p>subscribe-topic-listener</p></td></tr><tr><td rowspan="2"><p>sap</p></td><td><p>document-listener</p></td></tr><tr><td><p>function-listener</p></td></tr><tr><td><p>servicebus</p></td><td><p>listener</p></td></tr><tr><td><p>sftp</p></td><td><p>listener</p></td></tr><tr><td><p>sockets</p></td><td><p>listener</p></td></tr><tr><td rowspan="2"><p>solace</p></td><td><p>queue-listener</p></td></tr><tr><td><p>topic-listener</p></td></tr><tr><td rowspan="2"><p>sqs</p></td><td><p>receive-messages</p></td></tr><tr><td><p>receivemessages</p></td></tr><tr><td rowspan="3"><p>stripe</p></td><td><p>citizen-on-new-charge-listener</p></td></tr><tr><td><p>on-new-charge-listener</p></td></tr><tr><td><p>on-new-event-listener</p></td></tr><tr><td><p>vm</p></td><td><p>listener</p></td></tr><tr><td rowspan="2"><p>websocket</p></td><td><p>inbound-listener</p></td></tr><tr><td><p>outbound-listener</p></td></tr></tbody></table>

#### Mule Flows Generated by APIkit and Used for Routing APIkit Requests

APIkit is a tool that simplifies API implementation by automatically generating a minimal set of
Mule flows based on the API specification. Each APIkit router endpoint counts as a distinct Mule
flow. These Mule flows donât have an event source and are used to HTTP requests for a particular API
method and path.

This flow routes APIkit requests and handles the GET request in the `/reservation` path:

```xml
<flow name="get:\reservation:cocheras-puerto-madero-api-config">
        <logger level="INFO" message="#[output json --- attributes.queryParams]" />
</flow>
```

#### Mule Flows That Donât Count Against Your Anypoint Platform Package Allotment

Mule flows that donât have an event source and arenât used for routing APIkit requests arenât
charged against your Anypoint Platform package allotment. These Mule flows are primarily used to
modularize code.

For example:

```xml
2.a - Flow with only a logger component
<flow name="just-logging">
        <logger level="INFO" message="#[output json --- attributes.queryParams]" />
</flow>
```

### Total Mule Messages

A Mule message is the data (the payload and its attributes) that passes through one or more Mule
flows in an application. A Mule message is part of a Mule event, which is generated when the event
source within a Mule flow is triggered. For example, a Mule event that consists of a Mule message is
created when an HTTP Listener receives a request or each time the Scheduler component triggers an
execution of the Mule flow.

Mule message processors in a Mule flow (such as core components, file read operations, or the HTTP
request operations) can then retrieve, set, and process Mule message data that resides in the Mule
event according to their configurations.

A Mule message is immutable, so every change to a Mule message results in the creation of a new
instance. Each processor in a flow that receives a Mule message returns a new Mule message that
consists of a message payload (the body of the message) and message attributes (metadata associated
with the message).

#### Mule Message Scenarios That Count Toward Your Anypoint Platform Package Allotment

When an event source within a flow of a Mule application is triggered, the event source such as HTTP
Listener or Scheduler, generates a Mule event that encapsulates a Mule message. The Mule message
generated by the event source counts toward your Anypoint Platform package allotment. Any new
instances of that message created during the processing of the original message as it moves through
other processors in connected Mule flows, donât count toward your Anypoint Platform package
allotment.

### Total Data Throughput

Data throughput is all of the network I/O bytes produced by the infrastructure for the Mule runtime
server that runs a Mule application. This includes the data that the application produces to execute
its business logic, as well as internal operational network traffic such as logs, health checks, and
monitoring traffic. For example, data throughput includes inserting a record into a database and the
network traffic associated with the app infrastructure, such as log forwarding, control plane
connection, and monitoring metrics transfer.

## Object Store Usage

Object Store v2 usage reports show this information in tables and cards:

Business Group

Business group that owns the resources where the app with the object store is deployed

Environment

Environment within the business group where the app with the object store is deployed

Region id

Region where the app with the object store is deployed

Store

Name of the object store associated with the API requests

Effective API requests

API requests along with the payload size and number of keys returned, used to calculate units
against the quota

Total Effective API requests

Sum of effective API requests that return a 2xx (success) response across all object stores in the
organization

## Runtime Fabric Usage Metrics

The usage dashboard for Runtime Fabric lets you track, monitor, and govern core usage in hybrid
environments and provides detailed metrics and analysis for Runtime Fabric instances and application
deployments.

You can see your cluster capacity metrics refined by business group. To see CPU limit aggregates at
the business group level, [export the usage report as a CSV](usage-reports#export-usage-reports).
The CPU limit is shown at the application level on the usage dashboard, along with the associated
business groups, which helps you identify which applications use the most CPU, so you can adjust
your application configurations if needed.

The dashboard helps you understand your usage and associated pricing by letting you compare cores
and flows consumed by each application side-by-side. You can visualize core and flows consumed by
each application alongside each other. Export the usage report to a CSV file to visualize cores and
flows consumed by each app and drill down per environment ID and business group.

If youâre using the cluster capacity metric for billing, this translation from core usage to flow
usage enables you to make the necessary changes to applications so you can transition to usage-based
pricing iteratively, which gives you a more flexible pricing model that scales with your integration
needs and isnât tied to your infrastructure.

### Mule Version Requirements for Flow Metrics

Alongside the cluster capacity and CPU limit, you can also view other usage metrics (such as flow,
messages, and data throughput). Ensure that the Mule applications are using the compliant
[Supported Mule Runtime Versions](usage-reports#supported-mule-runtime-versions).

### Runtime Fabric Agent Version and Configuration Requirements

If you are using the cluster capacity metric for tracking, additional configuration requirements
apply. These requirements donât apply for accurate tracking of the CPU limit metric.

- The Runtime Fabric agent must be version 2.7.0 or later.

  For more information, see [Upgrading Runtime Fabric](../runtime-fabric/latest/upgrade-index).

- If you are using a Helm-based Runtime Fabric installation, ensure that the `NODE_WATCHER`
  parameter is enabled.

  For more information about enabling `NODE_WATCHER`, see
  [Optional Parameters](../runtime-fabric/latest/install-helm#optional-parameters).

These configurations only apply to the Runtime Fabric agent and have no impact on applications. You
donât need to take any action on applications (redeployment or restarts) for tracking the cluster
capacity and CPU limit metrics.

### Runtime Fabric Compliance Tracking With Usage Metrics

You must adjust your clusters and application configurations for accurate usage-metric measurement.
You can still use the CPU limit metric or cluster capacity metric at the root organization level for
contract governance depending on your use case.

These recommendations can help you accurately measure metrics:

<table><colgroup><col> <col></colgroup><thead><tr><th>CPU Limit</th><th>Cluster Capacity</th></tr></thead><tbody><tr><td><div><div><ul><li><p>Each application requires variable CPU allocation depending on its workload requirements. Use Anypoint Monitoring to analyze the CPU usage of your application during development to identify the right CPU limit and CPU reserve configurations.</p></li><li><p>Runtime Manager sets the CPU limit configuration to the available cluster capacity of the underlying Runtime Fabric instance by default, which can result in a higher CPU limit usage reported for applications if the CPU limit configuration isnât explicitly modified during deployment. For an accurate CPU limit metric, ensure that the applicationâs CPU limit configurations are updated with the appropriate values based on the appâs performance.</p></li></ul></div></div></td><td><div><div><ul><li><p>You can install Runtime Fabric instances in a shared Kubernetes cluster, with both namespace isolation and node isolation for applications and Runtime Fabric core service workloads. These isolations arenât reflected by the cluster capacity metric captured from the Kubelet server. To use this metric for compliance, use a dedicated cluster for Runtime Fabric instances for accurate measurements.</p></li><li><p>If a cluster is reconfigured with hyper-threading, virtualization of compute cores, or has system nodes for advanced cluster management, MuleSoft canât account for the usage impact of these configurations on the cluster capacity metric. If you must have these configurations and use the cluster-capacity metric, contact your account team for accurate compliance audits.</p></li></ul></div></div></td></tr></tbody></table>

For more information, see
[MuleSoft License Compliance](../runtime-fabric/latest/limitations-self#license-compliance).

### Maximum CPU Limit in Production

You can drill down and track CPU limit based usage per application ID and group them using this
metadata:

- Runtime Fabric cluster name

  Tracks whether the underlying cluster has enough capacity to scale.

- Business group

  Shows usage per business group.

- Root organization

  Monitor core usage to stay under the total entitlement.

  The cluster-capacity metric is available only at the root organization level.

The maximum CPU limit in production is the maximum amount of CPU an app can use when deployed and
running in the production runtime plane. The CPU amount is shared on the worker node. Each member of
a set of workers for the same app can have a different value for the CPU limit. Autoscale policies,
if implemented, can execute every 30 minutes, which can impact the worker or replica count and CPU
limit of an app deployment.

Data is captured every hour but the max metric is tracked on a daily and monthly basis as shown in
this example.

### Example

Here is an example with two apps:

App1 has a CPU limit of one each and a worker count range of 1 - 3.

App2 has a CPU limit of one each and a worker count range of 2 - 5.

Data captured at 00:00 minutes:

- App1: sum(1,1,1) = 3
- App2: sum(1,1,1,1,1) = 5
- Max metric captured = 8 CPU limit aggregated

Redeployment of App1 with a CPU limit of 2 and worker range of 1 - 6 at 00:45 minutes:

- App1: sum(1,1,1,2,2,2) = 9
- App2: sum(1,1,1,1,1) = 5

Time 01:00 min:

- App1: sum(2,2,2,2,2,2) = 12
- App2: sum(1,1,1,1,1) = 5
- Max metric captured = 17 CPU limit aggregated
- For the root organization in a given hour (not reported on the usage dashboard):

  Max concurrent limit CPU = 17

- For the root organization in a given day:

  Max concurrent limit CPU = 17

- For the root organization in a given month:

  Max concurrent limit CPU = 17

App2 auto scales down to three workers after 02:00 min:

- App1: sum(2,2,2,2,2,2) = 12
- App2: sum(1,1,1) = 3
- Max metric = 15 CPU limit aggregate
- For the root organization in a given hour (not reported on the usage dashboard):

  Max concurrent limit CPU = 15

- For the root organization in a given day:

  Max concurrent limit CPU = 17

- For the root organization in a given month:

  Max concurrent limit CPU = 17

### Maximum CPU Limit in Preproduction

The maximum CPU limit in preproduction (sandbox) is the maximum amount of CPU an app can use when
deployed and running in preproduction.

You can drill down and track CPU limit-based usage per application ID and group them using metadata,
including:

- Runtime Fabric cluster name

  Tracks whether the underlying cluster has enough capacity to scale.

- Business group

  Shows usage per business group.

- Root organization

  Monitor core usage to stay under the total entitlement.

  The cluster capacity metric is available only at the root organization level.

Data is captured every hour, but the max metric is tracked daily and monthly as shown in this
[Example](#example).

When an application is moved from a preproduction to a production environment, the CPU limit
configuration for that application is accounted for in the daily max meter of both preproduction and
production environments.

### Maximum Cluster Capacity in Production

Your maximum cluster capacity depends on your cluster configuration.

Data is captured every hour, but the max metric is tracked daily and monthly as shown in this
[Example](#example).

Note that the CPU capacity of a single node is derived from the `Allocatable:cpu:` field when you
describe the node. The total cluster CPU capacity is the sum of all these `Allocatable:cpu:` values
in millicores from the worker nodes. These worker nodes are those that donât have these labels
`node-role.kubernetes.io/master`, `node-role.kubernetes.io/control-plane`,
`node-role.kubernetes.io/ingress`, or `rtf.mulesoft.com/ingress`.

See [Cluster Configurations](../runtime-fabric/latest/rtf-cluster-config).

### Maximum Cluster Capacity in Preproduction

Your maximum cluster capacity depends on your cluster configuration.

Data is captured every hour, but the max metric is tracked daily and monthly as shown in this
[Example](#example).

Note that the CPU capacity of a single node is derived from the `Allocatable:cpu:` field when you
describe the node. The total cluster CPU capacity is the sum of all these `Allocatable:cpu:` values
in millicores from the worker nodes. These worker nodes are those that donât have these labels
`node-role.kubernetes.io/master`, `node-role.kubernetes.io/control-plane`,
`node-role.kubernetes.io/ingress`, or `rtf.mulesoft.com/ingress`.

See [Cluster Configurations](../runtime-fabric/latest/rtf-cluster-config).

### Exclude Nodes From Runtime Fabric Cluster Capacity

For nodes not intended to run Mule apps, exclude the nodes from your Runtime Fabric clusterâs
capacity report by following these steps:

1.  Apply the label `rtf.mulesoft.com/non-worker` to the node. This prevents further Mule apps to be
    scheduled on that node.
2.  Make sure that no Mule apps are currently running on the node. The node is excluded from the
    capacity report if no Mule apps are detected.
3.  Make sure the Runtime Fabric agent version is 2.10.0 or higher.

> [!NOTE] Use a dedicated cluster for Runtime Fabric instances for accurate measurements

## See Also

- [Usage Reports](usage-reports)

# Usage Reports Release Notes

> For the full documentation index, see: https://docs.mulesoft.com/llms.txt

Each Anypoint Platform organization subscribes to a specific pricing, usage, and packaging model.
Some functionality within Anypoint Platform depends on your organizationâs setup and provisioning.

The following release notes are provided for pricing, usage, and packaging:

- [Usage Reports Release Notes](../release-notes/usage-reports/usage-reports-release-notes)
---

# Usage Reports

> For the full documentation index, see: https://docs.mulesoft.com/llms.txt

Usage reports provide comprehensive monthly and daily summaries that give you valuable insights into
your resource use across Anypoint Platform products. By correlating your usage with billing cycles,
you can gain insights into the factors influencing your costs. This enables you to proactively
manage your budget and make informed decisions to optimize your resource allocation.

When you use an Anypoint Platform product that is metered, MuleSoft measures your usage and
aggregates the records on a daily and monthly basis.

Usage report data is refreshed twice a day. The most recent three days of data is subject to change.
For example, on January 10, data from January 7 to January 10 might still be updated. Data from
January 6 and earlier is final and wonât change.

All reports are calculated using the UTC time standard. For example, a daily usage is aggregated
from 00:00 to 00:00 UTC.

Daily usage retention is three months and monthly usage retention is up to five years.

Organizations that have a usage-based pricing model can compare actual usage from the report against
the existing contract. However, usage reports are not for billing purposes. For more information
about usage metrics and pricing, read [Anypoint Platform Pricing](pricing) and
[Usage and Pricing Metrics Reference](usage-metrics)

Some usage data, such as monthly statements and vCore allocation, appears in different areas of
Anypoint Platform or Salesforce products.

- Monthly statements for Mule runtime, including Mule flows, Mule messages, and data throughput are
  sent to the primary billing contact.

  [Automation credits](automation-credits-usage-and-rates) for RPA, Composer, Intelligent Document
  Processing (IDP), Salesforce Flow Orchestration, and MuleSoft for Flow are sent to the primary
  billing contact.

  For more information, see
  [Monthly Account Summaries](https://help.salesforce.com/s/articleView?id=000390885&type=1)
  documentation in Salesforce.

- Usage of certain Salesforce generative AI features (Agentforce and Einstein generative AI) or
  direct calls to the large language model (LLM) gateway consumes credits. All Salesforce AI usage
  from Anypoint Platform features is routed through your connected Salesforce org, which tracks
  consumption and handles billing.

  Anypoint Platform features that use Einstein generative AI or Agentforce features include:
  - Anypoint Code Builder

    Use MuleSoft Dev Agent to develop integrations and API specs.

    For more information about this feature, see
    [Developing Integrations Using MuleSoft Vibes](../anypoint-code-builder/int-ai-developing-integrations)
    and [MuleSoft Vibes Overview](../anypoint-code-builder/mulesoft-vibes).

  - Intelligent Document Processing (IDP)

    Use Einstein generative AI to read and interpret documents.

    For more information about this feature, see
    [Enhancing Data Extraction with Einstein](../idp/enhancing-data-extraction-with-einstein)..

  - Anypoint Exchange

    Use Einstein generative AI to generate API documentation for REST APIs.

    For more information about this feature, see
    [Generating API Documentation with Generative AI](../exchange/generating-documentation-with-ai).

    For more information about how the usage for these features is tracked and billed, see
    [Agentforce and Generative AI Usage and Billing](https://help.salesforce.com/s/articleView?id=ai.generative_ai_usage.htm&type=5).

- Alerts for usage reaching 80% and 90% of the contractual entitlement are sent automatically to the
  primary billing contact email address. The primary billing contact gets alerts monthly once
  thresholds are reached.

  For more information, see the
  [Salesforce Usage Alerts FAQ](https://help.salesforce.com/s/articleView?id=004811177&type=1)
  knowledge article.

- Runtime Manager vCore allocation information and other metrics appear in Access Management.

  For more information, see [Runtime Manager Metrics](usage-metrics#runtime-manager-metrics).

Government Cloud and Anypoint Private Cloud Edition usages arenât available in usage reports.

## Products Available in Usage Reports

The following products are available in usage reports:

- Anypoint MQ
- API Experience Hub
- API Governance
- API Manager
- Omni Gateway
- Omni Gateway LLM Proxy
- Intelligent Document Processing (IDP)
- Mule Runtime
- Object Store v2

## Supported Mule Runtime Versions

To view usage reports for Mule runtime, all applications (in production and sandbox) must use these
Mule versions:

<table><colgroup><col> <col></colgroup><thead><tr><th>Deployment Type</th><th>Version Requirements</th></tr></thead><tbody><tr><td><p>CloudHub 2.0</p></td><td><p>The latest date-patch versions for 4.3.0 and later</p></td></tr><tr><td><p>CloudHub</p></td><td><p>The latest date-patch versions for 3.9 and 4.2.0 and later</p></td></tr><tr><td><p>Runtime Fabric</p></td><td><div><div><p>One of these versions or any later date-patch:</p></div><div><p>Mule 4 image versions:</p></div><div><ul><li><p>4.5.0 and later</p></li><li><p>4.4.0-20230111-1</p></li><li><p>4.3.0-20221212-2</p></li><li><p>4.2.2-20221027-7</p></li><li><p>4.2.1-20221027-7</p></li><li><p>4.2.0-20221027-6</p></li></ul></div></div></td></tr><tr><td><p>Hybrid Standalone</p></td><td><div><div><p>One of these versions or any later date-patch:</p></div><div><ul><li><p>4.6.0 and later</p></li><li><p>4.4.0-20221111</p></li><li><p>4.3.0-20221024</p></li></ul></div><div><p>Runtime Manager Agent 2.7.0 and later</p></div></div></td></tr></tbody></table>

You must redeploy your apps with a supported version, either manually or during the monthly patching
cycle. The monthly patching cycle is applicable only for CloudHub and CloudHub 2.0 applications.

You can [roll back](../cloudhub-2/ch2-update-apps#roll-back-to-a-previous-successful-configuration)
to a previous successful application configuration. However, if an app is using a Mule version not
supported by usage reports, you must choose a compliant Mule version before deploying changes. After
deployment, a new configuration with the previously chosen settings and a supported runtime is
created.

## Before You Begin

Usage reports are available only when youâre signed in to the root organization and donât appear at
the business group level.

To access usage reports, you need the
[Usage Viewer permission](../access-management/permissions-by-product#usage). If you donât have this
permission, contact your organization administrator to request access. The Usage Viewer permission
must be [applied at the root organization level](../access-management/managing-permissions) rather
than the business group level.

## Access Usage Reports

1.  Sign in to Anypoint Platform using an account that has the Usage Viewer permission for your root
    organization.
2.  In the Anypoint Platform navigation bar, click the business group selector, and ensure you are
    at the root organization level.
3.  In the Anypoint Platform navigation bar, click your username icon and select **Usage Reports**.
4.  Select the product for which to view a usage report.
5.  Click **Monthly Usage Report** or **Daily Usage Report**.
6.  Select the month or day of the usage report to view. For the selected product, only dates that
    Anypoint Platform collected data for are available.

    The usage report appears, displaying an assortment of usage metrics that are
    [dependent on the product you selected](usage-metrics).

## Export Usage Reports to a CSV File

You can export both daily and monthly usage reports to CSV files. These metrics match the data
available in usage reports.

1.  Navigate to the usage report to download.
2.  Click **Export as CSV**.

## Usage API

Use the Anypoint Usage API to programmatically access usage data. The API provides both daily and
monthly details. Usage metrics are organized by business group and application. This provides
detailed insight into product consumption.

To use the API, see the API documentation for the
[Anypoint Usage API](https://anypoint.mulesoft.com/exchange/portals/anypoint-platform/f1e97bc6-315a-4490-82a7-23abe036327a/usage-api/)
for access and implementation details. Users must have Usage Viewer permission to access the Usage
API.

## Usage Reports on Control Planes

All features of Usage Reports are supported on Japan and Canada control planes.

## See Also

- [Anypoint API Governance Overview](../api-governance/)
- [API Manager 2.x](../api-manager/latest/)
